content.text: O3A alignment and fidelity ranking complete. Now the per-analog overlay figure. I'll load the figure-style skill for the publication-grade deliverable and render 3D-aligned overlays as PCA-projected 2D skeletons (parent in grey, analog in colour).

content.content:
<skill-metadata name="figure-style" source="anthropic" note="read-only — fork under a new name to modify" />

# Publication-Grade Figure Rules

*A checklist for correct, legible, internally-consistent scientific figures. This
skill does not impose a visual house style — frame, font, and palette are
parameters. Once loaded, call `apply_figure_style()` before plotting.*

## §0 Scope

**Load trigger** (same rule as the system prompt's "Publication-grade plots"
section): this skill is for **final-deliverable figures** — those shipping in a
report, paper, or export, or saved as an artifact the user will keep — not for
exploratory/intermediate plots (quick looks, EDA, sanity checks), which are
drawn plainly without it. Once loaded, "every plot" below means every plot you
render toward the deliverable.

§1–§3, §8, and §9 are **correctness** — they apply to every plot, in every
context, and have no aesthetic content. §4–§7 are **guidance** — defaults that
produce a clean result but that a deliberate alternative can override
(individual rules inside §4–§7 that state a factual/perceptual invariant — e.g.
§4.4 semantic-zero centering, §4.5 CVD, §6.9 leader anchoring — still bind). On
its own, this skill is the inner tier (make one plot good); `figure-composer`
and `paper-narrative` supply multi-panel and whole-paper context.

---

## §1 Data fidelity & self-consistency

**1.1 Excluded rows.** A row marked excluded or flagged in the source data is
either omitted entirely or drawn with a visually distinct open/hatched marker
and named in the key. It **never** enters a summary statistic plotted alongside
the included rows.

**1.2 Comparable conditions only.** Arms measured under non-comparable
conditions (different N, epoch budget, initialization, protocol) are not plotted
as visual peers. Separate them with a facet break or a marker on the label, and
state the difference once in the caption.

**1.3 Self-consistency.** Every key, threshold, and title inside the figure must
be satisfied by every plotted row. Before saving, walk each categorical outcome
label back to the rule that defines it; if a row's value contradicts its label
or the title, the figure is wrong, not the data.

**1.4 Claim-titles must be true.** A sentence-title (§5.1) is tested against
every category on the axis before rendering. If any contradicts it, qualify the
title ("on 3 of 4 pairs") or downgrade it to a description.

**1.5 State n and what was held fixed.** Every panel that draws a summary mark
states `n` and the unit of replication, and every small-multiple that holds a
variable fixed states the fixed value — in the panel or, when §2 budget is
tight, in the caption.

**1.6 Reference structure is reference.** A tree, ordering, or topology drawn as
*context* (a scale bar, a category strip) uses an established reference, not
one inferred from the plotted data. Infer the structure only when the structure
*is* the result.

**1.7 One number per claim.** A quantitative claim (runtime, accuracy, count)
has exactly one canonical value across every panel, caption, and the abstract.
Define what it measures and use that value everywhere.

---

## §2 Label economy — floor and ceiling

The figure shows the pattern; the **caption** carries the context. Design for a
general scientific reader, not the author.

**2.1 Floor (non-removable).** Every distinct mark, series, glyph, or comparator
must be identifiable from the figure alone. The caption explains *why it
matters*, not *what it is*. A label is non-removable if deleting it leaves a
reader asking "what is that?"; it is removable only if the question becomes "why
is that there?". Comparator labels name the thing ("prior method", "no joint
training"), never a bare role word ("baseline", "previous"). Any term a general
scientist can't parse gets a one-word gloss.

**2.2 Ceiling.** Per panel: title + axis labels + tick labels + series identity
(labeled once per row of small multiples) + at most 2–3 result annotations.
Count the strings; >6 beyond axes/ticks means you're over. The ceiling counts
*narrative* annotations (callouts, value labels, brackets) — identity labels are
floor, not budget.

**2.3 Move to the caption:** n=, what's-held-fixed, abbreviation expansions,
non-comparable footnotes, exclusion rationale, methodological caveats.

**2.4 Titles are takeaways.** A reader seeing only the title knows what the
panel shows. "Robust to gene dropout" passes; "Fewer genes" fails. Test: read it
aloud cold — if the listener asks "fewer genes *what*?", rewrite. For a row of
small multiples that vary one thing, drop per-panel titles for one row-header.

**2.5 Value-on-mark only for the headline number** — the one a reader would
quote. Everything else is read off the axis.

**2.6 When in doubt, delete the label and re-read.** If the message survives, it
stays deleted.

---

## §3 Axes, scales, small multiples

**3.1 Axis padding.** Axis limits clear the data by ≥ one marker radius on every
side; markers and text never touch a spine. `ax.margins(0.04)` after plotting,
or extend the limit past any annotation.

**3.2 Axis breaks over wasted range.** When data occupy <40 % of an axis, break
the axis or start it at the data floor with a clear non-zero tick. Never draw a
reference line, threshold, or annotation inside a broken-axis gap — the gap has
no coordinate.

**3.3 Log axes get human-readable ticks** — `10²`, `10³`, or `1k / 10k / 100k`,
not raw exponents. **Never** draw filled bars on a log-scaled value axis (bar
length encodes ratio to an arbitrary floor); use points + median tick instead.

**3.4 Shared axes across small multiples.** A row or column of small multiples
shows tick labels once (leftmost / bottommost panel); interior panels keep ticks
but drop labels. When the panels share a y-axis and differ only by x-variable,
render them as abutting subplots (`wspace≤0.06`) with one row-header title.

**3.5 Fill the box.** A panel's data envelope occupies ≥75 % of its allotted
rectangle. If a panel's natural aspect leaves dead bands, reshape the grid
(rowspan, stacked complementary panels) — don't pad the panel.

**3.6 Direction of goodness.** When higher- or lower-is-better is not obvious
from the axis label, place a small upright cue ("higher = better") in the
margin — once per row of panels, never per panel, and never only in the caption.
A directional glyph embedded in rotated text rotates with it; set the cue
upright.

**3.7 Physical width.** A single-row figure at 300 dpi fits the venue's
double-column width. Adding a schematic or labels does not push data panels narrower than
they were before.

---

## §4 Color

**4.1 Threading.** Once a color is bound to an entity (a method, a feature, a
condition), reuse that exact color for every mark representing that entity
across the figure — line, fill, marker, text, heatmap row. Color *is* the
cross-reference; a reader should never have to consult a legend twice.

**4.2 Limit hues.** Use as few distinct hues as the data require. When the
figure compares a focal series against others, make the focal series visually
dominant (saturated, heavier weight) and render comparators with lower visual
weight (desaturated, lighter, or thinner). The focal hue must not coincide with
any hue in a categorical palette used in the same figure. The focal series must
remain identifiable even when its mark is zero-width or coincident with others —
via outline, marker, or a light tinted band.

**4.3 Hierarchical categories.** When categories nest (groups within groups),
the outer level picks the hue family and the inner level samples within it.

**4.4 Continuous and diverging.** Use a perceptually uniform sequential map for
generic continuous values; a single-hue ramp for ordinal rank or size; a
diverging map for signed quantities — **always** centered at the semantically
meaningful zero (0, 1.0, or median), never the data midpoint.

**4.5 CVD safety.** Never rely on a red/green contrast for a binary or opposing
distinction. Any binary pair should remain distinguishable in deuteranopia
simulation. Reserve one alarm hue for error/anomaly/perturbation marks and do
not reuse it as a data-series color.

**4.6 Two palettes, two legends.** When a figure uses two categorical color
systems, each legend sits adjacent to the first panel where its palette applies.

---

## §5 Typography

**5.1 Sentence titles.** A panel title states the comparison in plain language,
regular weight, left-aligned. Metric names go on the axis, not in the title.

**5.2 Role-mapped size ladder.** A figure uses **at most three** font sizes,
mapped to *role* not space: titles/axis-labels/series-identity at the base size;
legend/annotation text one step down; tick labels one step further. Panel
letters are the only exception (bold, larger). If a label doesn't fit at its
role's size, fix the layout or shorten the text — don't reach for an
intermediate size. `apply_figure_style(sizes=(8,7,6))` sets the ladder.

**5.3 Nomenclature.** Species, gene, and variable names that scientific
convention italicizes are italicized. Abbreviated codes inherit the rule; expand
once on first appearance.

**5.4 Magnitude suffixes.** Large counts use `k / M / B` (`4.2B`, `120 kb`),
not comma-grouped full numerals.

**5.5 Numeric annotations.** On-mark numbers use at most 2 significant figures —
unless 2-sf rounding would make two distinct rows print the same value, in which
case show the digit that separates them. Text on a filled mark reaches ≥4.5:1
contrast; if it doesn't, place the text outside the mark.

**5.6 No internal codes.** Axis labels use plain-language names; codebase
abbreviations appear only in parentheses after the readable name or in the
caption. Comparator series are labeled with what they *are*, not a role word.

**5.7 Panel letters.** Bold, top-left, outside the axes box. Case follows the
target venue's convention; `panel_letter(ax, 'a', case=...)` handles either.

---

## §6 Chart-family guidance (by data shape)

**6.1 Categorical × numeric.** Show the distribution, not just the summary.
Chart choice follows n: jittered strip with a median tick for small n; box or
violin for large n; bar + overlaid raw points or bar + interval when the mean is
the message. The `errorbar='ci95'` interval is the t-distribution 95% CI of the
mean (half-width `t_{0.975,n−1}·s/√n`), so it is valid at small n. Error bars
and raw-point overlays are alternatives — showing both is usually redundant. A
category absent from a group is marked (`n.d.`, `—`, or a hatched ghost) at its
slot; an empty slot reads as zero. A zero-valued bar
gets a visible stub or dot at the baseline.

**6.2 Single-observation categories.** A filled dot with a thin neutral stem to
the semantic zero (lollipop). Value labels sit beside the dot.

**6.3 Continuous series.** Mean-per-x as a line with markers; individual runs as
thin translucent lines or points behind it. Label each series with direct text
at the right end of its line in preference to a legend box. Summary glyphs
(per-bin mean/median) use a shape that cannot be mistaken for a raw observation,
identical across series, drawn below the raw points in z-order.

**6.4 Distributions on shared support.** When two distributions overlap heavily,
stack them as small panels with a shared x-axis or use a ridgeline. Overlay only
when the separation is visually clear.

**6.5 Matrices.** When a heatmap is small enough to read (< ~200 cells), print
the value in every cell. State the threshold once in the colorbar label.

**6.6 Embeddings.** Dimensionality-reduction scatters (UMAP, t-SNE, PCA) drop
ticks and tick labels; a small corner arrow pair names the axes. Clusters are
labeled by thin leader lines to text in surrounding whitespace.

**6.7 Paired prediction vs. observation.** Stack the two as adjacent tracks with
identical x and color; let the alignment carry the comparison. Target regions
are translucent spans registered in the legend.

**6.8 Insets.** Connect a detail inset to its source region visibly — a bounding
box with connector lines, or a translucent wedge.

**6.9 Label the extremes.** On a scatter of named observations, direct-label at
least the maximum, minimum, and any flagged point with a thin leader line. After
rendering, verify every leader endpoint terminates within one marker radius of
the row it names.

---

## §7 Layout & narrative

**7.1 Show what is measured before the result.** A reader should grasp what's
being compared before seeing the comparison — via a plain-language title, a
labeled schematic, or panel ordering. Any schematic uses the same words and
glyphs as the data panels' labels.

**7.2 One figure, one message.** A multi-panel figure has a single sentence it
is trying to make true. Every panel either states it, supports it, or bounds it;
panels that do none of these belong in supplement.

**7.3 Legends live in whitespace.** Frameless, placed inside the figure's
natural whitespace, or replaced by direct labeling. Legend entries are
swatch-first, left-aligned, and resolve every visually distinct glyph on the
panel.

**7.4 Row-band headers for nested faceting.** When small multiples are grouped,
each group gets one spanning header, not repeated per-panel titles.

**7.5 The figure arc.** For a paper: Figure 1 renders the paper's one-sentence
pitch as data — scope, not architecture. Subsequent figures cover mechanism,
evidence, robustness, application. A panel is judged against the paper's pitch,
not just its own figure's claim; content moves between figures if that's where
the story needs it. (`paper-narrative` runs this review.)

**7.6 Don't re-decorate a passing panel.** Between revision rounds, a panel that
already passes is not made more visually complex to fix nothing. Adding marks or
labels to a clean panel is a regression.

---

## §8 Anti-patterns

These are correctness failures, not style preferences:

- Red and green as opposing categories.
- Filled bars on a log-scaled value axis.
- Colorbar ticks that are evenly spaced but miss the semantic center.
- A diverging colormap whose center is the data midpoint, not the semantic zero.
- An axis title that restates the tick labels.
- Explaining the direction of goodness only in the caption.
- A "reference" line drawn at a value that is itself one of the plotted points.
- An excluded row that enters a plotted summary statistic.
- A leader line whose nearest mark is not the row it labels.

---

## §9 Render-then-verify

After `fig.savefig(...)`, before `save_artifacts`:

**9.1 Geometric (bbox) check.**
```python
r = fig.canvas.get_renderer()
texts = [(t, t.get_window_extent(r)) for t in fig.findobj(mpl.text.Text)
         if t.get_text().strip() and t.get_visible()]
spines = [(s, s.get_window_extent(r)) for ax in fig.axes
          for s in ax.spines.values() if s.get_visible()]
ticklabels = {ax: set(ax.get_xticklabels(which='both') + ax.get_yticklabels(which='both'))
              for ax in fig.axes}
overlaps  = [(a, b) for i, (a, ba) in enumerate(texts) for b, bb in texts[i+1:] if ba.overlaps(bb)]
overlaps += [(t, s) for t, bt in texts for s, bs in spines
             if bt.overlaps(bs) and t not in ticklabels[s.axes]]
# assert: overlaps == [] and every text box lies within fig.bbox
```
Overlap is defined between *visible* boxes, and a tick label sitting on its own
spine is not a finding. Fix (move, shorten, stagger) and re-save until clean.

**9.2 Perceptual check.** The bbox check is geometric, not perceptual — it will
not catch a low-contrast label, a leader that crosses three others, or a series
color mistakable for another. Crop the saved PNG to each panel and look:
```python
fig.savefig("figure.png")
for letter, box in panel_crops(fig).items():
    host.view_image("figure.png", crop=box)
```
For each crop: Is every glyph and mark legible against its background? Does the
smallest plotted element have a stroke or stub? Do any leaders cross? Could any
series color be mistaken for another? Does the legend sit beside what it keys?
A perceptual defect that passes §9.1 is still a defect.

---
*When in doubt: fewer hues, more direct labels, raw data over summary stats, and
state what is being measured before showing the result.*

input.code:
import os, json
os.makedirs("qm", exist_ok=True)

# 5 linkage variants
qm_set = ["Lac-Phe (parent)","Thioamide (C=S)","Reduced amide (CH2-NH)","N-Me amide","1,2,3-triazole"]

def scissile(m, name):
    """Return (atomA, atomB) 0-based defining the scissile bond and label."""
    if "riazole" in name:
        # scissile-equivalent = C(alpha)-N(triazole) bond (the bond that replaces amide C-N)
        patt = Chem.MolFromSmarts("[CX4]n1ccnn1")
        mm = m.GetSubstructMatches(patt)
        return (mm[0][0], mm[0][1]), "Calpha-Ntriazole"
    elif "Reduced" in name:
        patt = Chem.MolFromSmarts("[CX4][NX3;H1,H2][CX4]")
        mm = m.GetSubstructMatches(patt)
        return (mm[0][0], mm[0][1]), "CH2-NH"
    else:
        patt = Chem.MolFromSmarts("[CX3](=[O,S])[NX3]")
        mm = m.GetSubstructMatches(patt)
        return (mm[0][0], mm[0][2]), "C(=X)-N"

meta = {}
def safe(name): return name.replace(" ","_").replace("(","").replace(")","").replace("-","_").replace(",","")
for name in qm_set:
    m_full, best, en = mol_objs[name]
    m = single_conf_mol(m_full, best)
    (a,b), label = scissile(m, name)
    fn = f"qm/{safe(name)}.xyz"
    Chem.MolToXYZFile(m, fn, confId=0)
    # charge is 0 (neutral acid form). xtb 1-based indices:
    meta[name] = dict(xyz=fn, scissile_1based=[a+1,b+1], label=label,
                      atomA_sym=m.GetAtomWithIdx(a).GetSymbol(),
                      atomB_sym=m.GetAtomWithIdx(b).GetSymbol(),
                      natoms=m.GetNumAtoms())
json.dump(meta, open("qm/meta.json","w"), indent=2)
for n,d in meta.items():
    print(f"{n:24s} scissile {d['label']:16s} atoms {d['atomA_sym']}{d['scissile_1based'][0]}-{d['atomB_sym']}{d['scissile_1based'][1]} (natoms {d['natoms']})")

input.human_description: Parsing Wiberg bond orders and linkage charges
input.code:
import json, numpy as np
meta = json.load(open("qm/meta.json"))
def safe(name): return name.replace(" ","_").replace("(","").replace(")","").replace("-","_").replace(",","")

def parse_wbo(path):
    d={}
    for line in open(path):
        p=line.split()
        if len(p)>=3:
            i,j,w=int(p[0]),int(p[1]),float(p[2])
            d[(i,j)]=w; d[(j,i)]=w
    return d
def parse_charges(path):
    return [float(x) for x in open(path) if x.strip()]

qm_rows=[]
for name,d in meta.items():
    base=safe(name)
    rd=f"qm/run_{base}"
    wbo=parse_wbo(f"{rd}/wbo")
    chg=parse_charges(f"{rd}/charges")
    a,b=d['scissile_1based']
    bo=wbo.get((a,b),float('nan'))
    qA,qB=chg[a-1],chg[b-1]
    # geometry retention: RMSD of opt vs input heavy atoms
    qm_rows.append(dict(molecule=name, linkage=d['label'],
        scissile_atoms=f"{d['atomA_sym']}{a}-{d['atomB_sym']}{b}",
        wiberg_bond_order=round(bo,3),
        q_atomA=round(qA,3), q_atomB_N=round(qB,3),
        charge_sep=round(abs(qA-qB),3)))

import pandas as pd
qdf=pd.DataFrame(qm_rows)
# rank by bond order (higher = more covalent/inert scissile bond)
qdf=qdf.sort_values('wiberg_bond_order',ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
print(qdf.to_string(index=False))

content.text: The raw bond order alone is misleading — it ranks thioamide first, but the genuinely inert isosteres (reduced amide, triazole) show C–N ~1.0 precisely because they've *removed* the hydrolyzable carbonyl. Let me build a chemically-reasoned verdict that separates "amide resonance strength" from "hydrolytic inertness."

input.code:
# Classify each linkage: does it retain a hydrolyzable carbonyl/thiocarbonyl electrophile?
carbonyl = {
 "Lac-Phe (parent)": "amide C=O (hydrolyzable)",
 "N-Me amide": "tertiary amide C=O (hydrolyzable)",
 "Thioamide (C=S)": "thioamide C=S (hydrolyzable, modified electronics)",
 "Reduced amide (CH2-NH)": "NONE - secondary amine (no scissile carbonyl)",
 "1,2,3-triazole": "NONE - aromatic heterocycle (no scissile carbonyl)",
}
# Inertness rank reasoned from chemistry, not bond order alone:
# no-carbonyl isosteres are non-hydrolyzable; within amide series use electrophilicity + BO
inert_rank = {
 "1,2,3-triazole": 1,
 "Reduced amide (CH2-NH)": 2,
 "Thioamide (C=S)": 3,
 "N-Me amide": 4,
 "Lac-Phe (parent)": 5,
}
verdict = {
 "1,2,3-triazole": "GENUINELY higher-barrier. Amide replaced by aromatic 1,2,3-triazole; no electrophilic carbonyl exists for water/peptidase attack. Metabolically inert. Best hydrolytic stability. C-N BO ~0.98 (true single bond) is NOT a liability here.",
 "Reduced amide (CH2-NH)": "GENUINELY higher-barrier. Carbonyl removed -> secondary amine; scissile bond is an sp3 C-N single bond (BO 1.01), not a hydrolysis substrate. Non-hydrolyzable, but introduces a basic protonatable amine (charge/pharmacophore change).",
 "Thioamide (C=S)": "MODESTLY more inert than amide. Highest C-N bond order (1.39 - strongest amide-like resonance / rotational barrier) and least electrophilic scissile carbon (q=+0.14 vs +0.26). Notably resistant to enzymatic (peptidase) cleavage; still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.",
 "N-Me amide": "MARGINAL. Still a hydrolyzable amide (BO 1.33, electrophilic carbon q=+0.25); N-methylation gives only steric/entropic protection and blocks the donor N-H. Not a true stability fix.",
 "Lac-Phe (parent)": "BASELINE liability. Classic secondary amide: electrophilic carbonyl carbon (q=+0.26), C-N BO 1.32, ground-state resonance modest. The bond the whole project aims to protect.",
}

qdf['carbonyl_electrophile'] = qdf['molecule'].map(carbonyl)
qdf['inertness_rank'] = qdf['molecule'].map(inert_rank)
qdf['hydrolytic_verdict'] = qdf['molecule'].map(verdict)
qdf = qdf.sort_values('inertness_rank').reset_index(drop=True)

cols = ['inertness_rank','molecule','linkage','scissile_atoms','wiberg_bond_order',
        'q_atomA','q_atomB_N','charge_sep','carbonyl_electrophile','hydrolytic_verdict']
qdf = qdf[cols]
qdf.to_csv("hydrolytic_stability_qm.csv", index=False)

content.text: QM ranking complete. Now the markdown findings note, then save all artifacts.

input.code:
note = r"""# Phase 1a Findings — Lac-Phe Analog Pharmacophore Fidelity + Hydrolytic-Stability QM

**Goal:** identify Lac-Phe analogs that (i) preserve the 3D pharmacophore of the parent
N-lactoyl-L-phenylalanine and (ii) resist hydrolysis of the central scissile linkage.
Target-free, CPU-only. 7 molecules: parent amide + 6 analogs.

## Methods (brief)
- **Conformers:** RDKit ETKDGv3, 50 embeddings, 0.5 Å RMSD pruning, MMFF94 minimization; global-min conformer kept. Per-molecule ensemble SDFs saved.
- **Fidelity:** RDKit Open3DAlign (O3A, MMFF) of each analog's best conformer onto the parent's; shape Tanimoto (1 − ShapeTanimotoDist); 3D displacement of the three SAR-critical groups (terminal carboxylate C, lactoyl secondary OH, central linkage N) after alignment. Composite `fidelity_score` = 0.4·shapeT + 0.4·(inverse key-group deviation) + 0.2·O3A, min-max normalized across analogs.
- **QM stability:** GFN2-xTB, geometry optimization with implicit water (`--alpb water`), neutral acid form, full molecules (32–35 atoms; all converged). Scissile-bond Wiberg bond order + Mulliken charges on the linkage atoms.

## Pharmacophore fidelity (ranked)
| Rank | Analog | shapeT | O3A | mean key-group dev (Å) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | alpha-Me-Phe | 0.58 | 114 | 0.45 | ⚠ **recognition risk** (see below) |
| 2 | 1,2,3-triazole | 0.60 | 93 | 1.76 | loses amide N-H donor + carbonyl; lactoyl-OH swings ~3.9 Å |
| 3 | D-Phe | 0.50 | 105 | 0.86 | inverted α-center throws carboxylate ~2.4 Å off-parent |
| 4 | Thioamide (C=S) | 0.41 | 96 | 0.47 | near-isosteric geometry; C=S weaker acceptor, N-H stronger donor |
| 5 | N-Me amide | 0.43 | 81 | 1.06 | loses the central amide N-H **donor** (tertiary amide) |
| 6 | Reduced amide (CH2-NH) | 0.48 | 86 | 4.27 | carbonyl acceptor lost + flexible basic amine; poor overlay |

**⚠ alpha-Me-Phe recognition flag:** its 3D overlay is the best of the set (all three key
groups within ~0.45 Å, highest O3A), but this is a geometric artifact. Published SAR on
amino-acid / taste (T1R) receptors shows that methylating the α-hydrogen — creating a
quaternary α-carbon — sharply reduces receptor affinity because the α-C·H / α-amino
recognition motif is blocked. **A good overlay does not rescue this liability; treat
alpha-Me-Phe as high recognition risk despite its top fidelity score.**

## Hydrolytic stability (GFN2-xTB, ranked most→least inert)
| Rank | Linkage | scissile C–N Wiberg BO | scissile C charge | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,2,3-triazole | 0.98 | +0.01 | **Genuinely inert** — no carbonyl electrophile; aromatic heterocycle. Best. |
| 2 | Reduced amide (CH2-NH) | 1.01 | ~0.00 | **Genuinely inert** — carbonyl removed; sp3 C–N single bond is not a hydrolysis substrate. |
| 3 | Thioamide (C=S) | 1.39 | +0.14 | Modestly more inert — highest C–N double-bond character, least electrophilic scissile C; resists peptidase cleavage but retains hydrolyzable C=S. |
| 4 | N-Me amide | 1.33 | +0.25 | Marginal — still a hydrolyzable amide; only steric protection. |
| 5 | Lac-Phe (parent) | 1.32 | +0.26 | Baseline liability — electrophilic amide carbonyl. |

**Important interpretation:** raw scissile-bond order alone is *misleading*. Thioamide has the
highest C–N bond order (1.39, strong amide-like resonance) yet still carries a hydrolyzable
thiocarbonyl. The two backbone replacements that show C–N ≈ 1.0 (triazole, reduced amide) are
the genuinely higher-barrier isosteres precisely because they **eliminate the electrophilic
carbonyl** — hydrolysis needs a C=O/C=S to attack, and neither has one. So the descriptor is
read in two regimes: *within the amide-retaining series* (parent/N-Me/thioamide) higher BO +
lower carbonyl-carbon charge = slower attack (thioamide > N-Me ≈ parent); *the no-carbonyl
isosteres sit in a different class entirely* and are non-hydrolyzable.

## Combined read — best candidates
- **1,2,3-triazole** — the only analog that is both a top-2 pharmacophore match (highest shape
  Tanimoto, 0.60) **and** the most hydrolytically inert. Cost: loses the amide N-H donor and the
  lactoyl-OH position shifts ~3.9 Å; worth checking whether the OH/donor vectors matter once a
  receptor hypothesis exists. **Strongest all-round candidate.**
- **Thioamide** — near-isosteric geometry (key groups within 0.5 Å) with a real, if modest,
  electronic stabilization and good enzymatic resistance. Conservative pick that changes shape
  least; stability gain is incremental, not dramatic.
- **Reduced amide** — fully hydrolysis-inert but the worst pharmacophore match (4.3 Å mean
  key-group deviation, new basic amine). Stability without recognition — likely too disruptive.
- **alpha-Me-Phe** — do **not** advance on fidelity score alone; α-methylation is a known
  recognition-killer (flagged).
- **N-Me amide / D-Phe** — N-Me gives little stability and loses a donor; D-Phe keeps chemistry
  but the inverted stereocenter displaces the carboxylate. Lower priority.

**Recommendation for Phase 1b:** prioritize **triazole** (stability + shape) and **thioamide**
(minimal shape perturbation) for any downstream target-based or MD work; carry reduced-amide as
a stability control; deprioritize alpha-Me-Phe, N-Me, and D-Phe.

*Caveats:* single global-min conformer used for alignment (not full-ensemble O3A); MMFF/GFN2-xTB
are approximate (no explicit hydrolysis transition state computed — bond order/charge are
ground-state proxies for lability, not activation barriers); neutral acid form modeled (carboxylate
is anionic at physiological pH); recognition claims are SAR-literature-based, not from a validated
Lac-Phe receptor.
"""
open("phase1a_findings.md","w").write(note)
print("wrote", len(note), "chars")

content.text:
All three steps are complete and the structured output is submitted. Here's the summary.

## Phase 1a complete — pharmacophore fidelity + hydrolytic-stability QM for 7 Lac-Phe analogs

**Step 1 — Conformer ensembles.** ETKDGv3 (50 embeds, 0.5 Å pruning) + MMFF94 minimization for all 7 molecules; energy-sorted ensembles saved (global-min first).

**Step 2 — Pharmacophore fidelity vs parent.** O3A alignment onto the parent's best conformer, shape Tanimoto, and 3D displacement of the three SAR-critical groups (carboxylate, lactoyl-OH, central linkage N).

![Analog overlays on Lac-Phe]({{artifact:499328b6-4019-4a41-8a38-38b59c0212c4}})

**Step 3 — GFN2-xTB stability** (`--opt --alpb water`, full molecules, all converged). Scissile C–N Wiberg bond order + linkage charges, interpreted in two regimes.

| Inertness | Linkage | C–N Wiberg BO | scissile C charge | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,2,3-triazole | 0.98 | +0.01 | **Genuinely inert** — no carbonyl electrophile |
| 2 | Reduced amide | 1.01 | ~0.00 | **Genuinely inert** — sp3 C–N, no carbonyl |
| 3 | Thioamide | 1.39 | +0.14 | Modestly more inert; retains C=S |
| 4 | N-Me amide | 1.33 | +0.25 | Marginal — still hydrolyzable |
| 5 | Parent amide | 1.32 | +0.26 | Baseline liability |

**Key conclusions:**
- **1,2,3-triazole** is the strongest all-round candidate — top-2 shape match (shapeT 0.60) *and* the most hydrolytically inert.
- **Thioamide** is the most shape-conservative isostere (key groups within 0.5 Å) with a real but modest electronic stabilization.
- Raw bond order alone is misleading: the carbonyl-free isosteres show C–N ≈ 1.0 precisely because they've removed the hydrolyzable electrophile — that's the point, not a liability.
- **alpha-Me-Phe flagged:** best raw overlay, but α-H methylation is a known recognition-killer for amino-acid/taste receptors — not advanced on fidelity alone.

Artifacts saved:
- [pharmacophore_fidelity.csv]({{artifact:e7d995d9-b344-47e6-ab9a-a4acd61e87e2}}) — ranked fidelity table with per-group deviations and the recognition flag
- [pharmacophore_overlays.png]({{artifact:499328b6-4019-4a41-8a38-38b59c0212c4}}) — per-analog overlay grid
- [hydrolytic_stability_qm.csv]({{artifact:82722927-b3f2-4aad-8ce9-1671dbb9df5d}}) — xTB scissile-bond descriptors and verdicts
- [phase1a_findings.md]({{artifact:87092d92-248c-44e5-b6ae-01e4a67f763b}}) — full findings note with combined recommendation
- [conformer_ensembles.tgz]({{artifact:76568731-131b-48e6-8612-26e6d26da369}}) — 7 per-molecule SDF ensembles

[Auditor] <verification_findings starts="0,48">
A fresh-context reviewer traced your work and found 2 issues:

  1. [fail] "pharmacophore_fidelity.csv donor/acceptor count for N-Me amide contradicts its own feature-match note"
     Reading the saved CSV (host.artifact_path on e7d995d9-b344-47e6-ab9a-a4acd61e87e2): the N-Me amide row has donors=3, acceptors=4 — identical to the parent's reference feature counts printed in msg[15] ('REF features: {...Donor: 3, Acceptor: 4...}'). Yet the same row's feature_match_notes column (and the identical sentence repeated in phase1a_findings.md, version 87092d92-248c-44e5-b6ae-01e4a67f763b) states 'Retains COOH+OH+aromatic; loses one amide N-H DONOR (tertiary amide) - central H-bond donor vector lost.' The SMILES for N-Me amide (C[C@H](O)C(=O)N(C)[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O) is indeed a tertiary amide with no N-H, so chemically a donor should be lost (3->2), but the computed donors column stayed at 3, contradicting the note in the same saved row. All other rows' donor/acceptor counts are internally consistent with their notes (e.g. thioamide acceptors 3 vs parent 4 matches 'weaker acceptor'; reduced amide acceptors 3 vs 4 matches 'loses carbonyl ACCEPTOR'; triazole donors 2 vs 3 matches 'loses N-H donor') — only the N-Me amide row's numeric donor count fails to reflect the claimed donor loss, meaning the saved donors/acceptors feature-count deliverable contains an incorrect/inconsistent value for this analog, directly bearing on the task's explicit request to quantify preservation of the central amide H-bond donor/acceptor vectors.
  2. [warn] "phase1a_findings.md / submitted top_findings state alpha-Me-Phe's 3 key SAR groups are 'all... within ~0.45 Å' / '<0.45 A', but its own per-group data shows the linkage-N deviation is 0.71 Å"
     pharmacophore_fidelity.csv (e7d995d9) row for alpha-Me-Phe: carboxylate_dev_A=0.32, lactoylOH_dev_A=0.31, linkageN_dev_A=0.71, mean_key_group_dev_A=0.45. The 0.45 is the MEAN, not a per-group ceiling, yet phase1a_findings.md states "all three key groups within ~0.45 Å" and the submitted output's top_findings repeats "all key groups <0.45 A" for alpha-Me-Phe. The CSV's own feature_match_notes field for this row also only cites 2 of 3 groups ("carboxyl 0.3A, OH 0.3A") while omitting the 0.71 Å linkage-N outlier, i.e. the claim of near-perfect 3-group overlay is not supported by the row's own linkageN_dev_A value. This does not reverse the recommendation (alpha-Me-Phe is still correctly flagged as a recognition risk and not advanced), so it is a presentation/precision issue in a saved artifact rather than a conclusion-changing error.

Acknowledge in one line and make the fix (or rebut in one line if a finding is wrong). Don't restate or narrate your evaluation.

            dons.append(f"{a.GetSymbol()}{aid}(H{a.GetTotalNumHs()})")
    print(f"{nm:20s} donors={len(dons)}: {dons}")

content.text: Confirmed — RDKit's donor SMARTS flags the tertiary amide N (H0) as a donor despite having no N–H to donate. The physically correct definition requires ≥1 attached H on the donor heavy atom. Let me apply that filter and rebuild the fidelity table.

input.human_description: Recomputing H-filtered donor counts and rebuilding fidelity CSV
input.code:
# Physically correct H-bond donor = donor heavy atom bearing >=1 H
def da_counts(smi):
    m = Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi)
    feats = factory.GetFeaturesForMol(m)
    don=acc=0
    for f in feats:
        fam=f.GetFamily(); aid=f.GetAtomIds()[0]; a=m.GetAtomWithIdx(aid)
        if fam=="Donor" and a.GetTotalNumHs()>=1: don+=1
        elif fam=="Acceptor": acc+=1
    return don,acc

da = {nm:da_counts(smi) for nm,smi in mols.items()}
print("Corrected H-filtered D/A:")
for nm,(d,a) in da.items(): print(f"  {nm:26s} D={d} A={a}")

# Rebuild the fidelity dataframe donors/acceptors from corrected counts
for i,row in df.iterrows():
    d,a = da[row['analog']]
    df.at[i,'donors']=d; df.at[i,'acceptors']=a

# alpha-Me-Phe note: cite all 3 groups incl the 0.71 A linkage-N; fix "all within 0.45"
notes["alpha-Me-Phe"] = ("Carboxylate 0.32A and lactoyl-OH 0.31A near-perfectly overlaid; "
    "linkage-N further off at 0.71A (mean key-group dev 0.45A). H-bond D/A intact. "
    "Overlay is strong but see recognition flag.")
notes["N-Me amide"] = ("Retains COOH+OH+aromatic; N-methylation removes the amide N-H DONOR "
    "(donors 3->2), losing the central H-bond donor vector.")
df['feature_match_notes']=df['analog'].map(notes)
df.to_csv("pharmacophore_fidelity.csv", index=False)

chk = df[['analog','donors','acceptors','carboxylate_dev_A','lactoylOH_dev_A','linkageN_dev_A','mean_key_group_dev_A']]
print(chk.to_string(index=False))

Both findings addressed and corrected output resubmitted.

1. **N-Me amide donor count (blocking):** RDKit's donor SMARTS was flagging the tertiary amide N as a donor even with no N–H present. Applied an H-count filter (donor heavy atom must bear ≥1 H) — N-Me amide now correctly reads donors=2, consistent with its "loses the amide N-H donor" note. All other rows unchanged.

2. **alpha-Me-Phe overlay wording (warn):** clarified in both the CSV note and findings markdown that 0.45 Å is the *mean* of the three groups — carboxylate 0.32 Å and lactoyl-OH 0.31 Å overlay tightly, but the linkage-N sits further off at 0.71 Å. The recommendation is unchanged (still flagged as recognition risk, not advanced).

Updated artifacts: [pharmacophore_fidelity.csv]({{artifact:3c625feb-b444-432b-aed3-392cfbb0ad04}}) (v2) and [phase1a_findings.md]({{artifact:3c3ce7f5-6597-4ed2-b9b5-b159cec81f57}}) (v2).

input_data.request:
Phase 2: candidate-target docking screen for 7 Lac-Phe analogs (CPU only, smina/AutoDock Vina). The
true receptor is UNKNOWN, so the goal is DIFFERENTIAL — does each analog reproduce the PARENT's binding mode
and key contacts in a given pocket — NOT to trust absolute cross-chemotype docking scores.

The 7 ligands (name: SMILES):
  "Lac-Phe (parent)": "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "N-Me amide": "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N(C)[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "alpha-Me-Phe": "C[C@H](O)C(=O)NC(C)(Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "D-Phe": "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N[C@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "Reduced amide (CH2-NH)": "C[C@H](O)CN[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "Thioamide (C=S)": "C[C@H](O)C(=S)N[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O"
  "1,2,3-triazole": "OC(=O)[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)n1cc([C@H](O)C)nn1"

A curated, scored candidate-target panel from a prior session is here: {/Users/sgh2449/.claude-science/orgs/cc20f312-d1a2-46f7-92bc-7c54df515458/artifacts/proj_e08ff4f59eb4/ea082bfa-c893-4eeb-82c0-9a46e2bff78d/v76fb433a_lacphe_candidate_targets.csv}
(lacphe_candidate_targets.csv — columns include target, protein, uniprot, role, ligand_plausibility score).

STEP 1 — Assemble receptor panel. From that CSV, select the DIRECT-BINDING / ligand-plausible receptors
(s_ligand_plausibility >= 1 and role is a sensor/receptor, NOT pure downstream effectors like KCNJ11/ABCC8 or
transporters SLC17A1/3). This gives GPCR-class candidates such as CASR, HCAR1 (GPR81), GPRC6A, NPY1R, MC4R,
TAS1R1, TAS1R3, GPR171, MRGPRD, OR51E1. For each, obtain a structure: prefer an experimental PDB of the
human receptor with a bound orthosteric ligand (best for defining the box); else use the AlphaFold DB model
(https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/files/AF-<UNIPROT>-F1-model_v4.pdb). Cap the panel at ~8-10 receptors to stay
tractable. Record which came from PDB vs AlphaFold (AlphaFold pockets are lower-confidence — note it).

STEP 2 — Prep. Protonate receptors and ligands at physiological pH (carboxylate anionic). Define the docking
box at the known orthosteric/ligand-binding pocket where a holo PDB exists; for AlphaFold-only targets, center
on the pocket suggested by conserved orthosteric residues (or use blind/whole-domain docking and say so).
Use meeko/OpenBabel for ligand prep, smina (pip/conda installable) for docking.

STEP 3 — Dock all 7 ligands into every receptor (exhaustiveness ~8-16, ~9 poses each). Collect best score +
top poses per pair.

STEP 4 — Parent-contact consistency scoring. For each receptor, take the PARENT'S top pose as reference and
define its key interacting residues + the pharmacophore contacts (carboxylate salt-bridge partner, lactoyl-OH
H-bond, any pi-stack with the phenyl). Then for each analog score CONSISTENCY: does it dock into the same
pocket, reproduce the salt bridge, retain the H-bonds, overlap the parent pose (ligand RMSD or contact
Tanimoto)? Produce a per-(analog,target) consistency score (0-1) plus notes. This — not raw kcal/mol — is
the ranking axis.

DELIVERABLES (save as artifacts):
- docking_scores.csv (analog x target: best score, num poses)
- parent_contact_consistency.csv (analog x target: consistency 0-1, contacts reproduced, notes)
- receptor_panel.csv (target, uniprot, structure source PDB-id-or-AlphaFold, pocket definition, confidence)
- A shortlist (top compound-target pairs by consistency) for Phase 3 Boltz-2 confirmation, as shortlist_for_boltz.csv
- docking_figures.png (a few best parent-vs-analog pose overlays) — load figure-style skill for the deliverable figure
- phase2_findings.md (short summary; emphasize consistency ranking, flag AlphaFold-pocket caveats)
Return via output_schema. Actually run the docking; if a structure download is network-blocked, request access
or note which targets you could not fetch — do NOT fabricate poses or scores.
_desired_outputs: Pharmacophore-fidelity table + overlay figures (each analog vs Lac-Phe)
Candidate-target docking results with parent-contact consistency scores
Boltz-2 co-fold inputs (run_local.sh) + parsed affinity scores for survivors
Final ranked decision matrix + written report

output_data.response:
Both findings acknowledged and fixed against the artifact's own data:

1. **HCAR1 π-stack** — corrected to "all 6 reproduce the salt bridge; 5 of 6 also reproduce the His256 π-stack," noting N-Me-amide relocates its phenyl to Tyr263. Fixed in `phase2_findings.md` (now v2), the summary, and the top finding.
2. **Robustness ranking** — corrected to D-Phe (0.944) ≈ reduced-amide (0.943) > thioamide (0.933) > N-Me-amide (0.888) > α-Me-Phe (0.873) ≈ triazole (0.867), with the actual per-analog means, in both the report and the structured output.

Corrected output resubmitted. The underlying CSVs were already correct — only the prose claims that overstated the data have been brought into line with them.
_completion_bullets: Assembled 10-receptor panel (6 holo PDB + 4 AlphaFold)
Docked all 7 analogs x 10 receptors (70 smina runs, CPU)
Scored parent-contact consistency; HCAR1 & MRGPRD lead
Corrected HCAR1 pi-stack claim and robustness ranking from artifact data

structured_output.summary: Phase 2 differential docking screen: 7 Lac-Phe analogs (parent + 6 hydrolysis-resistant variants) docked with smina (CPU, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses each) into a 10-receptor panel drawn from the prior candidate-target CSV (ligand-plausible sensors/receptors only; effectors and transporters excluded). 6 receptors used experimental holo PDB structures with box centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand (CASR/7DTV L-Trp, HCAR1/8Z8A L-lactate, NPY1R/5ZBQ antagonist, MC4R/6W25 SHU9119, MRGPRD/7Y12 beta-alanine, TAS1R3/9NOR apo-TMD); 4 used AlphaFold DB v6 with fpocket-defined boxes (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1). All 70 dockings completed. Ranking axis is parent-contact CONSISTENCY (0-1) — pocket overlap + contact-residue Tanimoto + carboxylate salt-bridge reproduction + phenyl pi-stack reproduction — NOT raw cross-chemotype kcal/mol. HCAR1 (lactate receptor, direct chemotype match, holo pocket) is the standout: the parent forms a carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 and a pi-stack to His256, and all 6 analogs reproduce the salt bridge (5 of 6 also reproduce the His256 pi-stack; N-Me-amide relocates to Tyr263), consistency 0.87-1.00. MRGPRD is second (Arg82 salt bridge retained by all analogs). D-Phe and reduced-amide are the most parent-faithful analogs; the triazole is the most pocket-dependent (excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD, diverges at NPY1R/MC4R). The anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore across every salt-bridge-forming pocket.

artifacts.description: 10 receptors: UniProt, structure source (6 holo PDB + 4 AlphaFold v6), pocket definition, box, and pocket confidence (holo=high; AlphaFold/apo=low-moderate/low).
Analog x target best smina score (kcal/mol) and pose count (9 each). For pose ranking within a pair only — NOT comparable across receptors.

PRIMARY DELIVERABLE. Analog x target consistency (0-1) with centroid distance to parent pose, contact-residue Tanimoto, salt-bridge/pi-stack reproduction flags, partner residues, pocket confidence, and notes.
Top 10 compound-target pairs for Phase 3 Boltz-2, ranked by consistency x pocket-confidence x parent-pharmacophore richness (target-diversity capped at 4/receptor).
Consistency heatmap (7 analogs x 10 receptors, pocket-confidence flagged) + parent-vs-analog pose overlays for HCAR1, MRGPRD, NPY1R with key salt-bridge/pi-stack residues marked.
Narrative summary: method, consistency ranking, HCAR1/MRGPRD standouts, analog robustness ranking, and AlphaFold/apo-pocket caveats.

top_findings: HCAR1 (lactate receptor/GPR81, holo 8Z8A) is the most coherent hit: parent forms a carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 + pi-stack to His256 (same anchors lactate uses). ALL 6 analogs reproduce the salt bridge; 5 of 6 also reproduce the His256 pi-stack (consistency 0.87-1.00; D-Phe/thioamide/triazole/reduced-amide >=0.98). Exception: N-Me-amide keeps the salt bridge but relocates its phenyl to a different aromatic (Tyr263) rather than His256.
MRGPRD (beta-alanine sensor, holo 7Y12) is second: parent anchors carboxylate on Arg82; all analogs retain the salt bridge (0.89-0.97).

Analog parent-faithfulness ranking (mean consistency over the 5 high-confidence holo pockets CASR/HCAR1/NPY1R/MC4R/MRGPRD): D-Phe (0.944) ~ reduced-amide (0.943) > thioamide (0.933) > N-Me-amide (0.888) > alpha-Me-Phe (0.873) ~ triazole (0.867). D-Phe (stereochem-only change) is most conservative; triazole and alpha-Me-Phe are the least parent-faithful.
The triazole (largest scaffold change) is the most pocket-dependent: strong at HCAR1/MRGPRD/TAS1R3 but loses/relocates the phenyl pi-stack at NPY1R (0.69) and MC4R (0.76).

The anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore: in every pocket where the parent salt-bridges (HCAR1, MRGPRD, GPRC6A, GPR171, OR51E1), analogs keeping COO- keep the salt bridge — validating Phase 1's decision to preserve it.
caveats: True Lac-Phe receptor is unknown; this screen ranks which analogs behave like the PARENT per pocket, it does NOT identify the receptor.
Docking scores are not affinities; cross-chemotype kcal/mol are not comparable and were used only to rank poses within an (analog,target) pair.

AlphaFold pockets (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1) are low/low-moderate confidence (fpocket boxes, no experimental orthosteric ligand); high consistency there may reflect small/shallow pockets constraining all ligands similarly.
TAS1R3/9NOR has NO bound orthosteric ligand (druggability 0.001) - weakest pocket definition; the reduced-amide anomaly there (0.09) is likely pocket-definition noise.
Rigid-receptor docking, no induced fit - Boltz-2 co-folding in Phase 3 addresses this.

cands = {
 "CASR":  ["5K5T","5K5S","7M3E","7DTV"],

 "NPY1R": ["5ZBH","5ZBQ","7X9A"],
 "MC4R":  ["6W25","7F53","7PIU","7AUE"],
 "TAS1R3":["9NOR","9NOS","9O38"],
 "MRGPRD":["7Y12","7Y13","7Y14","7Y15"],
}
def info(pdb):
    r=requests.get(f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/entry/{pdb}",timeout=30)
    if r.status_code!=200: return None
    d=r.json()
    res=d.get("rcsb_entry_info",{}).get("resolution_combined",["?"])
    method=d.get("exptl",[{}])[0].get("method","?")
    ligs=d.get("rcsb_entry_info",{}).get("nonpolymer_bound_components") or []
    title=d.get("struct",{}).get("title","")
    return res,method,ligs,title
for name,lst in cands.items():
    print(f"=== {name} ===")
    for pdb in lst:
        i=info(pdb)
        if i: print(f"  {pdb}: {i[1]} {i[0]} ligs={i[2]}  {i[3][:70]}")

content.text: Let me identify the orthosteric ligand comp-IDs for the holo structures (the `nonpolymer_bound_components` field misses some agonists like lactate/β-alanine).

input.code:
chosen_holo = {"CASR":"7DTV","HCAR1":"8Z8A","NPY1R":"5ZBQ","MC4R":"6W25","TAS1R3":"9NOR","MRGPRD":"7Y12"}
for name,pdb in chosen_holo.items():
    r=requests.get(f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/entry/{pdb}",timeout=30).json()
    npids=r.get("rcsb_entry_info",{}).get("nonpolymer_entity_count",0)
    # get nonpolymer comp ids
    ligs=[]
    ne = r.get("rcsb_entry_container_identifiers",{}).get("non_polymer_entity_ids",[]) or []
    for eid in ne:
        rr=requests.get(f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/nonpolymer_entity/{pdb}/{eid}",timeout=30).json()
        cid=rr.get("pdbx_entity_nonpoly",{}).get("comp_id")
        nm=rr.get("pdbx_entity_nonpoly",{}).get("name")
        ligs.append((cid,nm))
    # polymer entities (peptide ligands)
    pe = r.get("rcsb_entry_container_identifiers",{}).get("polymer_entity_ids",[]) or []
    polys=[]
    for eid in pe:
        rr=requests.get(f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/polymer_entity/{pdb}/{eid}",timeout=30).json()
        nm=rr.get("rcsb_polymer_entity",{}).get("pdbx_description","")
        ln=rr.get("entity_poly",{}).get("rcsb_sample_sequence_length","?")
        polys.append((eid,nm,ln))
    print(f"{name} {pdb}: nonpoly={ligs}")
    print(f"      polymers={polys}")

content.text:
Clean orthosteric ligands identified for box definition:
- **CASR** 7DTV → TRP (L-tryptophan, aromatic AA site)
- **HCAR1** 8Z8A → 2OP (**L-lactate** — ideal for the lactoyl moiety)
- **NPY1R** 5ZBQ → 9AO (small-molecule antagonist)
- **MC4R** 6W25 → SHU9119 peptide (TM orthosteric pocket)
- **MRGPRD** 7Y12 → BAL (β-alanine, orthosteric AA)
- **TAS1R3** 9NOR → no bound orthosteric ligand (heterodimer, glycans only) → will need pocket detection, lower confidence

import os, requests
os.makedirs("dock/receptors_raw", exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs("dock/receptors", exist_ok=True)

# holo PDBs
holo = {"CASR":"7DTV","HCAR1":"8Z8A","NPY1R":"5ZBQ","MC4R":"6W25","TAS1R3":"9NOR","MRGPRD":"7Y12"}
for name,pdb in holo.items():
    r=requests.get(f"https://files.rcsb.org/download/{pdb}.pdb",timeout=60)
    open(f"dock/receptors_raw/{name}_{pdb}.pdb","wb").write(r.content)

# AlphaFold-only

    print(name,acc,r.status_code,len(r.content))

content.text: All 10 receptors are in hand. Now the critical prep step: for each holo structure I need to identify the receptor chain, extract the orthosteric reference ligand (for box definition), and strip G-proteins/Fabs/waters. Let me parse each structure.

input.code:
import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict

ref_lig = {"CASR":"TRP","HCAR1":"2OP","NPY1R":"9AO","MC4R":None,"TAS1R3":None,"MRGPRD":"BAL"}
# MC4R: peptide SHU9119 -> use its coords; TAS1R3: no ortho ligand

def parse_pdb(fn):
    atoms=[]  # (record, chain, resname, resseq, atomname, x,y,z)
    for line in open(fn):
        if line.startswith(("ATOM","HETATM")):
            atoms.append((line[:6].strip(), line[21], line[17:20].strip(),
                          line[22:27].strip(), line[12:16].strip(),
                          float(line[30:38]), float(line[38:46]), float(line[46:54])))
    return atoms

for name,pdb in holo.items():
    fn=f"dock/receptors_raw/{name}_{pdb}.pdb"
    at=parse_pdb(fn)
    # chain -> count of protein CA
    chain_ca=defaultdict(int); chain_res=defaultdict(set)
    het=defaultdict(int)
    for rec,ch,rn,rs,an,x,y,z in at:
        if rec=="ATOM" and an=="CA": chain_ca[ch]+=1
        if rec=="HETATM" and rn not in ("HOH","NAG","CA","NA","CL","ZN"): het[(ch,rn)]+=1
    print(f"{name} {pdb}: protein chains(CA counts)={dict(chain_ca)}")
    print(f"    hetgroups={dict(het)}")

input.code:
import numpy as np

# (pdb, receptor_chain, box_ref_selector)  box_ref_selector: (chain, resname) to center box on
holo_spec = {
 "CASR":   ("7DTV","A", ("A","TRP")),
 "HCAR1":  ("8Z8A","R", ("R","2OP")),
 "NPY1R":  ("5ZBQ","A", ("A","9AO")),
 "MC4R":   ("6W25","A", ("B","4J2")),  # buried aromatic of SHU9119
 "TAS1R3": ("9NOR","A", None),          # no ortho ligand -> fpocket
 "MRGPRD": ("7Y12","R", ("R","BAL")),
}

def read_lines(fn):
    return [l for l in open(fn)]

box_info={}
for name,(pdb,rchain,ref) in holo_spec.items():
    fn=f"dock/receptors_raw/{name}_{pdb}.pdb"
    lines=read_lines(fn)
    # write receptor: ATOM records for rchain only (drop HETATM, other chains, waters)
    out=[]
    refcoords=[]
    for l in lines:
        if l.startswith("ATOM") and l[21]==rchain:
            # skip altloc B
            alt=l[16]
            if alt not in (" ","A"): continue
            out.append(l)
        if ref and l.startswith("HETATM") and l[21]==ref[0] and l[17:20].strip()==ref[1]:
            refcoords.append((float(l[30:38]),float(l[38:46]),float(l[46:54])))
    open(f"dock/receptors/{name}_rec.pdb","w").writelines(out+["END\n"])
    if ref:
        c=np.array(refcoords)
        cen=c.mean(0); ext=c.max(0)-c.min(0)
        box_info[name]=dict(center=cen.round(2).tolist(),
                            size=np.clip(ext+8,18,26).round(1).tolist(),
                            ref=f"{ref[1]}/{ref[0]}", natoms=len(c))
        print(f"{name}: rec_atoms={len(out)} box center={box_info[name]['center']} size={box_info[name]['size']} ref={ref[1]}({len(c)} atoms)")
    else:
        print(f"{name}: rec_atoms={len(out)} -> fpocket needed")

for t in CASR HCAR1 NPY1R MC4R MRGPRD TAS1R3 GPRC6A TAS1R1 GPR171 OR51E1; do

  n=$(grep -c "^ATOM\|^HETATM" ${t}_rec.pdbqt 2>/dev/null)
  echo "$t: pdbqt atoms=$n  $(tail -1 /tmp/${t}.err)"
done

content.text: All receptors prepped. Now prepare the 7 ligands: generate 3D coords, protonate at pH 7.4 (carboxylate anionic), and make PDBQT. Let me build them from SMILES with RDKit for good 3D geometry, then convert.

input.code:
from rdkit import Chem
from rdkit.Chem import AllChem
from rdkit.Chem.MolStandardize import rdMolStandardize

ligands = {
 "parent":       "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "N-Me_amide":   "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N(C)[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "alpha-Me-Phe": "C[C@H](O)C(=O)NC(C)(Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "D-Phe":        "C[C@H](O)C(=O)N[C@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "Reduced_amide":"C[C@H](O)CN[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "Thioamide":    "C[C@H](O)C(=S)N[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)C(=O)O",
 "triazole":     "OC(=O)[C@@H](Cc1ccccc1)n1cc([C@H](O)C)nn1",
}

enol = AllChem.ETKDGv3()
enol.randomSeed = 42
for name,smi in ligands.items():
    m=Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi)
    # deprotonate carboxylic acid -> carboxylate at pH7.4
    m = rdMolStandardize.Reionizer().reionize(m)
    # enforce anionic carboxylate
    patt=Chem.MolFromSmarts("[CX3](=O)[OX2H1]")
    m2=Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi)
    mh=Chem.AddHs(m2)
    AllChem.EmbedMolecule(mh, enol)
    AllChem.MMFFOptimizeMolecule(mh, maxIters=500)
    Chem.MolToMolFile(mh, f"dock/ligands/{name}.mol")
    q=Chem.GetFormalCharge(m2)
    print(f"{name}: atoms={mh.GetNumAtoms()} charge(neutral form)={q}")

input.code:
import json
box_info=json.load(open("handoff/box_info.json"))
# write a job list
recs=["CASR","HCAR1","NPY1R","MC4R","MRGPRD","TAS1R3","GPRC6A","TAS1R1","GPR171","OR51E1"]
ligs=["parent","N-Me_amide","alpha-Me-Phe","D-Phe","Reduced_amide","Thioamide","triazole"]
jobs=[]
for t in recs:
    b=box_info[t]
    for lg in ligs:
        jobs.append((t,lg,b["center"],b["size"]))
json.dump(jobs, open("handoff/jobs.json","w"))
print(f"{len(jobs)} docking jobs queued ({len(recs)} receptors x {len(ligs)} ligands)")

input.command:
cd "$(pwd)/dock"
cat > run_dock.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# args: receptor ligand cx cy cz sx sy sz
t=$1; lg=$2; cx=$3; cy=$4; cz=$5; sx=$6; sy=$7; sz=$8
out=out/${t}__${lg}.pdbqt
log=out/${t}__${lg}.log
smina -r receptors/${t}_rec.pdbqt -l ligands/${lg}.pdbqt \
  --center_x $cx --center_y $cy --center_z $cz \
  --size_x $sx --size_y $sy --size_z $sz \
  --exhaustiveness 12 --num_modes 9 --cpu 2 --seed 42 \
  -o $out > $log 2>&1
echo "done ${t}__${lg}"
EOF
chmod +x run_dock.sh
echo "script ready"

content.text:
Panel table ready. Now let me write the analysis machinery for parent-contact consistency while docking runs. The approach: parse each output pose, extract the top pose per (target,ligand), compute the receptor residues contacted, identify pharmacophore contacts (carboxylate salt bridge to basic residue, lactoyl-OH H-bond, phenyl pi-stack), then score each analog's consistency vs the parent's reference pose.

Let me write the contact-analysis functions.

input.code:
analysis_code = '''
import numpy as np, os, re
from collections import defaultdict

RECS=["CASR","HCAR1","NPY1R","MC4R","MRGPRD","TAS1R3","GPRC6A","TAS1R1","GPR171","OR51E1"]
LIGS=["parent","N-Me_amide","alpha-Me-Phe","D-Phe","Reduced_amide","Thioamide","triazole"]

def parse_receptor(t):
    """return dict of atoms: list of (resname,resseq,chain,atomname,element,x,y,z)"""
    atoms=[]
    for l in open(f"dock/receptors/{t}_rec.pdbqt"):
        if l.startswith(("ATOM","HETATM")):
            rn=l[17:20].strip(); rs=l[22:27].strip(); ch=l[21]
            an=l[12:16].strip(); x,y,z=float(l[30:38]),float(l[38:46]),float(l[46:54])
            elem=l[77:79].strip() if len(l)>78 else an[0]
            atoms.append((rn,rs,ch,an,elem,x,y,z))
    return atoms

def parse_poses(t,lg):
    """return list of poses; each pose = (score, atoms) where atoms=list of (atomname,adtype,x,y,z)"""
    fn=f"dock/out/{t}__{lg}.pdbqt"
    if not os.path.exists(fn): return []
    poses=[]; cur=None; score=None
    for l in open(fn):
        if l.startswith("MODEL"):
            cur=[]; score=None
        elif l.startswith("REMARK") and "minimizedAffinity" in l:
            score=float(l.split()[-1])
        elif l.startswith(("ATOM","HETATM")) and cur is not None:
            an=l[12:16].strip(); ad=l[77:79].strip()
            x,y,z=float(l[30:38]),float(l[38:46]),float(l[46:54])
            cur.append((an,ad,x,y,z))
        elif l.startswith("ENDMDL"):
            poses.append((score,cur)); cur=None
    return poses

# basic (cationic) residues for salt bridge, and H-bond donor/acceptor atoms
BASIC_RES={"ARG":["NH1","NH2","NE"],"LYS":["NZ"],"HIS":["ND1","NE2"]}
AROM_RES={"PHE","TYR","TRP","HIS"}
HBOND_ACC={"O","N"}  # receptor side heavy atoms (crude)

def ligand_carboxylate_atoms(atoms):
    """OA oxygens that form the carboxylate (adtype OA); return their indices/coords"""
    return [(i,a[2:5]) for i,a in enumerate(atoms) if a[1]=="OA"]

def contacts_for_pose(t, pose_atoms, cutoff=4.0):
    rec=parse_receptor(t)
    reccoord=np.array([a[5:8] for a in rec])
    ligcoord=np.array([a[2:5] for a in pose_atoms])
    # residue-level contacts
    contacted=set()
    # per ligand-atom nearest residue
    for la in pose_atoms:
        lc=np.array(la[2:5])
        d=np.linalg.norm(reccoord-lc,axis=1)
        for j in np.where(d<cutoff)[0]:
            rn,rs,ch=rec[j][0],rec[j][1],rec[j][2]
            contacted.add((rn,rs,ch))
    return contacted, rec, reccoord

print("analysis functions defined")
'''
exec(analysis_code)

content.text: Now compute the parent reference for each receptor and score every analog's consistency. The consistency score combines: (a) same pocket / pose overlap (ligand centroid proximity to parent pose), (b) salt-bridge reproduced (same basic residue), (c) H-bond contact overlap (Tanimoto of contacted residues), (d) pi-stack reproduced.

Now score each analog's consistency. For each analog I evaluate all 9 poses and pick the one best reproducing the parent's contacts (the differential question: *can* this analog recover the parent mode).

input.code:
def tanimoto(a,b):
    a,b=set(a),set(b)
    if not a and not b: return 1.0
    return len(a&b)/len(a|b) if (a|b) else 0.0

def score_analog(t, lg):
    ref=parent_ref[t]
    poses=parse_poses(t,lg)
    ref_contacts=ref["ph"]["contact_res"]
    ref_sb=ref["ph"]["saltbridge"]; ref_pi=ref["ph"]["pistack"]
    ref_cen=ref["centroid"]
    best=None
    for score,atoms in poses:
        ph,ring=classify_pharmacophore(t,atoms)
        cen=pose_centroid(atoms)
        d_cen=np.linalg.norm(cen-ref_cen)
        tani=tanimoto(ref_contacts, ph["contact_res"])
        # components
        # pocket overlap: 1 if centroid within 4A, decaying to 0 at 12A
        pocket=float(np.clip(1-(d_cen-4)/8,0,1))
        # salt bridge: only relevant if parent had one
        if ref_sb is not None:
            sb=1.0 if ph["saltbridge"]==ref_sb else (0.5 if ph["saltbridge"] is not None else 0.0)
        else:
            sb=None
        # pi stack
        if ref_pi is not None:
            pi=1.0 if ph["pistack"]==ref_pi else (0.4 if ph["pistack"] is not None else 0.0)
        else:
            pi=None
        # composite weights: pocket 0.35, tanimoto 0.30, sb 0.20, pi 0.15 (renormalize if sb/pi absent)
        comps={"pocket":(pocket,0.35),"tani":(tani,0.30)}
        if sb is not None: comps["sb"]=(sb,0.20)
        if pi is not None: comps["pi"]=(pi,0.15)
        wsum=sum(w for _,w in comps.values())
        cons=sum(v*w for v,w in comps.values())/wsum
        rec=dict(cons=cons,pose_score=score,d_cen=d_cen,tani=tani,pocket=pocket,
                 sb=sb,pi=pi,ph=ph)
        if best is None or cons>best["cons"]: best=rec
    return best

# score all
cons_rows=[]
for t in RECS:
    for lg in LIGS:
        b=score_analog(t,lg)
        cons_rows.append(dict(analog=lg,target=t,consistency=round(b["cons"],3),
            best_pose_score=round(b["pose_score"],2),centroid_dist=round(b["d_cen"],2),
            contact_tanimoto=round(b["tani"],3),pocket_overlap=round(b["pocket"],2),
            saltbridge=b["sb"],pistack=b["pi"],
            sb_partner=str(b["ph"]["saltbridge"]),pi_partner=str(b["ph"]["pistack"])))
consdf=pd.DataFrame(cons_rows)
cpiv=consdf.pivot(index="analog",columns="target",values="consistency")[RECS].reindex(LIGS)
print(cpiv.round(2).to_string())

def make_note(row, t):
    ref=parent_ref[t]["ph"]
    parts=[]
    parts.append(f"pose@{row['centroid_dist']}Å" + (" (same pocket)" if row['centroid_dist']<5 else " (shifted)" if row['centroid_dist']<10 else " (DIFFERENT pocket)"))
    if row['sb_partner']!="None":
        parts.append("salt-bridge reproduced" if row['saltbridge']==1.0 else "salt-bridge to different residue")
    elif ref['saltbridge'] is not None:
        parts.append("salt-bridge LOST")
    if row['pi_partner']!="None":
        parts.append("π-stack reproduced" if row['pistack']==1.0 else "π-stack to different arom")
    elif ref['pistack'] is not None:
        parts.append("π-stack LOST")
    parts.append(f"contact-Tanimoto={row['contact_tanimoto']}")
    return "; ".join(parts)

consdf["notes"]=[make_note(r,r["target"]) for _,r in consdf.iterrows()]

# Rank compound-target pairs for Boltz confirmation.
# Prioritize: high-confidence pocket + parent pharmacophore richness + analog consistency + retains H-bond/salt-bridge.
# Exclude parent (it's the reference). Weight down AlphaFold/low-confidence pockets.
conf_weight={"high":1.0,"low-moderate":0.6,"low":0.4}
# parent pharmacophore richness per target (has salt bridge and/or pi-stack = mechanistically interpretable)
def target_relevance(t):
    ph=parent_ref[t]["ph"]
    r=0.0
    if ph["saltbridge"] is not None: r+=0.5   # carboxylate anchored
    if ph["pistack"] is not None: r+=0.3      # phenyl engaged
    r+=min(len(ph["hbond_res"]),5)/5*0.2
    return r

sub=consdf[consdf.analog!="parent"].copy()
sub["conf_w"]=sub.pocket_confidence.map(conf_weight)
sub["tgt_rel"]=sub.target.map(target_relevance)
# priority = consistency * pocket_confidence * (0.5 + 0.5*target_relevance)
sub["priority"]=(sub.consistency*sub.conf_w*(0.5+0.5*sub.tgt_rel)).round(3)
sub=sub.sort_values("priority",ascending=False)
shortlist=sub.head(12)[["analog","target","consistency","best_pose_score","priority","pocket_confidence","notes"]]
print(shortlist.to_string(index=False))

    sl.append(r); per_t[r.target]+=1
    if len(sl)>=10: break
shortdf=pd.DataFrame(sl)[["analog","target","consistency","best_pose_score","priority","pocket_confidence","sb_partner","pi_partner","notes"]]
shortdf.insert(0,"rank",range(1,len(shortdf)+1))
shortdf.to_csv("dock/shortlist_for_boltz.csv",index=False)
print(shortdf[["rank","analog","target","consistency","best_pose_score","priority","pocket_confidence"]].to_string(index=False))
pair_strings=[f"{r.analog} @ {r.target} (consistency={r.consistency}, {r.pocket_confidence}-conf pocket)" for _,r in shortdf.iterrows()]
json.dump(pair_strings, open("handoff/shortlist_pairs.json","w"))

<skill-metadata name="figure-style" source="anthropic" note="read-only — fork under a new name to modify" />

# Publication-Grade Figure Rules

*A checklist for correct, legible, internally-consistent scientific figures. This
skill does not impose a visual house style — frame, font, and palette are
parameters. Once loaded, call `apply_figure_style()` before plotting.*

## §0 Scope

**Load trigger** (same rule as the system prompt's "Publication-grade plots"
section): this skill is for **final-deliverable figures** — those shipping in a
report, paper, or export, or saved as an artifact the user will keep — not for
exploratory/intermediate plots (quick looks, EDA, sanity checks), which are
drawn plainly without it. Once loaded, "every plot" below means every plot you
render toward the deliverable.

§1–§3, §8, and §9 are **correctness** — they apply to every plot, in every
context, and have no aesthetic content. §4–§7 are **guidance** — defaults that
produce a clean result but that a deliberate alternative can override
(individual rules inside §4–§7 that state a factual/perceptual invariant — e.g.
§4.4 semantic-zero centering, §4.5 CVD, §6.9 leader anchoring — still bind). On
its own, this skill is the inner tier (make one plot good); `figure-composer`
and `paper-narrative` supply multi-panel and whole-paper context.

---

## §1 Data fidelity & self-consistency

**1.1 Excluded rows.** A row marked excluded or flagged in the source data is
either omitted entirely or drawn with a visually distinct open/hatched marker
and named in the key. It **never** enters a summary statistic plotted alongside
the included rows.

**1.2 Comparable conditions only.** Arms measured under non-comparable
conditions (different N, epoch budget, initialization, protocol) are not plotted
as visual peers. Separate them with a facet break or a marker on the label, and
state the difference once in the caption.

**1.3 Self-consistency.** Every key, threshold, and title inside the figure must
be satisfied by every plotted row. Before saving, walk each categorical outcome
label back to the rule that defines it; if a row's value contradicts its label
or the title, the figure is wrong, not the data.

**1.4 Claim-titles must be true.** A sentence-title (§5.1) is tested against
every category on the axis before rendering. If any contradicts it, qualify the
title ("on 3 of 4 pairs") or downgrade it to a description.

**1.5 State n and what was held fixed.** Every panel that draws a summary mark
states `n` and the unit of replication, and every small-multiple that holds a
variable fixed states the fixed value — in the panel or, when §2 budget is
tight, in the caption.

**1.6 Reference structure is reference.** A tree, ordering, or topology drawn as
*context* (a scale bar, a category strip) uses an established reference, not
one inferred from the plotted data. Infer the structure only when the structure
*is* the result.

**1.7 One number per claim.** A quantitative claim (runtime, accuracy, count)
has exactly one canonical value across every panel, caption, and the abstract.
Define what it measures and use that value everywhere.

---

## §2 Label economy — floor and ceiling

The figure shows the pattern; the **caption** carries the context. Design for a
general scientific reader, not the author.

**2.1 Floor (non-removable).** Every distinct mark, series, glyph, or comparator
must be identifiable from the figure alone. The caption explains *why it
matters*, not *what it is*. A label is non-removable if deleting it leaves a
reader asking "what is that?"; it is removable only if the question becomes "why
is that there?". Comparator labels name the thing ("prior method", "no joint
training"), never a bare role word ("baseline", "previous"). Any term a general
scientist can't parse gets a one-word gloss.

**2.2 Ceiling.** Per panel: title + axis labels + tick labels + series identity
(labeled once per row of small multiples) + at most 2–3 result annotations.
Count the strings; >6 beyond axes/ticks means you're over. The ceiling counts
*narrative* annotations (callouts, value labels, brackets) — identity labels are
floor, not budget.

**2.3 Move to the caption:** n=, what's-held-fixed, abbreviation expansions,
non-comparable footnotes, exclusion rationale, methodological caveats.

**2.4 Titles are takeaways.** A reader seeing only the title knows what the
panel shows. "Robust to gene dropout" passes; "Fewer genes" fails. Test: read it
aloud cold — if the listener asks "fewer genes *what*?", rewrite. For a row of
small multiples that vary one thing, drop per-panel titles for one row-header.

**2.5 Value-on-mark only for the headline number** — the one a reader would
quote. Everything else is read off the axis.

**2.6 When in doubt, delete the label and re-read.** If the message survives, it
stays deleted.

---

## §3 Axes, scales, small multiples

**3.1 Axis padding.** Axis limits clear the data by ≥ one marker radius on every
side; markers and text never touch a spine. `ax.margins(0.04)` after plotting,
or extend the limit past any annotation.

**3.2 Axis breaks over wasted range.** When data occupy <40 % of an axis, break
the axis or start it at the data floor with a clear non-zero tick. Never draw a
reference line, threshold, or annotation inside a broken-axis gap — the gap has
no coordinate.

**3.3 Log axes get human-readable ticks** — `10²`, `10³`, or `1k / 10k / 100k`,
not raw exponents. **Never** draw filled bars on a log-scaled value axis (bar
length encodes ratio to an arbitrary floor); use points + median tick instead.

**3.4 Shared axes across small multiples.** A row or column of small multiples
shows tick labels once (leftmost / bottommost panel); interior panels keep ticks
but drop labels. When the panels share a y-axis and differ only by x-variable,
render them as abutting subplots (`wspace≤0.06`) with one row-header title.

**3.5 Fill the box.** A panel's data envelope occupies ≥75 % of its allotted
rectangle. If a panel's natural aspect leaves dead bands, reshape the grid
(rowspan, stacked complementary panels) — don't pad the panel.

**3.6 Direction of goodness.** When higher- or lower-is-better is not obvious
from the axis label, place a small upright cue ("higher = better") in the
margin — once per row of panels, never per panel, and never only in the caption.
A directional glyph embedded in rotated text rotates with it; set the cue
upright.

**3.7 Physical width.** A single-row figure at 300 dpi fits the venue's
double-column width. Adding a schematic or labels does not push data panels narrower than
they were before.

---

## §4 Color

**4.1 Threading.** Once a color is bound to an entity (a method, a feature, a
condition), reuse that exact color for every mark representing that entity
across the figure — line, fill, marker, text, heatmap row. Color *is* the
cross-reference; a reader should never have to consult a legend twice.

**4.2 Limit hues.** Use as few distinct hues as the data require. When the
figure compares a focal series against others, make the focal series visually
dominant (saturated, heavier weight) and render comparators with lower visual
weight (desaturated, lighter, or thinner). The focal hue must not coincide with
any hue in a categorical palette used in the same figure. The focal series must
remain identifiable even when its mark is zero-width or coincident with others —
via outline, marker, or a light tinted band.

**4.3 Hierarchical categories.** When categories nest (groups within groups),
the outer level picks the hue family and the inner level samples within it.

**4.4 Continuous and diverging.** Use a perceptually uniform sequential map for
generic continuous values; a single-hue ramp for ordinal rank or size; a
diverging map for signed quantities — **always** centered at the semantically
meaningful zero (0, 1.0, or median), never the data midpoint.

**4.5 CVD safety.** Never rely on a red/green contrast for a binary or opposing
distinction. Any binary pair should remain distinguishable in deuteranopia
simulation. Reserve one alarm hue for error/anomaly/perturbation marks and do
not reuse it as a data-series color.

**4.6 Two palettes, two legends.** When a figure uses two categorical color
systems, each legend sits adjacent to the first panel where its palette applies.

---

## §5 Typography

**5.1 Sentence titles.** A panel title states the comparison in plain language,
regular weight, left-aligned. Metric names go on the axis, not in the title.

**5.2 Role-mapped size ladder.** A figure uses **at most three** font sizes,
mapped to *role* not space: titles/axis-labels/series-identity at the base size;
legend/annotation text one step down; tick labels one step further. Panel
letters are the only exception (bold, larger). If a label doesn't fit at its
role's size, fix the layout or shorten the text — don't reach for an
intermediate size. `apply_figure_style(sizes=(8,7,6))` sets the ladder.

**5.3 Nomenclature.** Species, gene, and variable names that scientific
convention italicizes are italicized. Abbreviated codes inherit the rule; expand
once on first appearance.

**5.4 Magnitude suffixes.** Large counts use `k / M / B` (`4.2B`, `120 kb`),
not comma-grouped full numerals.

**5.5 Numeric annotations.** On-mark numbers use at most 2 significant figures —
unless 2-sf rounding would make two distinct rows print the same value, in which
case show the digit that separates them. Text on a filled mark reaches ≥4.5:1
contrast; if it doesn't, place the text outside the mark.

**5.6 No internal codes.** Axis labels use plain-language names; codebase
abbreviations appear only in parentheses after the readable name or in the
caption. Comparator series are labeled with what they *are*, not a role word.

**5.7 Panel letters.** Bold, top-left, outside the axes box. Case follows the
target venue's convention; `panel_letter(ax, 'a', case=...)` handles either.

---

## §6 Chart-family guidance (by data shape)

**6.1 Categorical × numeric.** Show the distribution, not just the summary.
Chart choice follows n: jittered strip with a median tick for small n; box or
violin for large n; bar + overlaid raw points or bar + interval when the mean is
the message. The `errorbar='ci95'` interval is the t-distribution 95% CI of the
mean (half-width `t_{0.975,n−1}·s/√n`), so it is valid at small n. Error bars
and raw-point overlays are alternatives — showing both is usually redundant. A
category absent from a group is marked (`n.d.`, `—`, or a hatched ghost) at its
slot; an empty slot reads as zero. A zero-valued bar
gets a visible stub or dot at the baseline.

**6.2 Single-observation categories.** A filled dot with a thin neutral stem to
the semantic zero (lollipop). Value labels sit beside the dot.

**6.3 Continuous series.** Mean-per-x as a line with markers; individual runs as
thin translucent lines or points behind it. Label each series with direct text
at the right end of its line in preference to a legend box. Summary glyphs
(per-bin mean/median) use a shape that cannot be mistaken for a raw observation,
identical across series, drawn below the raw points in z-order.

**6.4 Distributions on shared support.** When two distributions overlap heavily,
stack them as small panels with a shared x-axis or use a ridgeline. Overlay only
when the separation is visually clear.

**6.5 Matrices.** When a heatmap is small enough to read (< ~200 cells), print
the value in every cell. State the threshold once in the colorbar label.

**6.6 Embeddings.** Dimensionality-reduction scatters (UMAP, t-SNE, PCA) drop
ticks and tick labels; a small corner arrow pair names the axes. Clusters are
labeled by thin leader lines to text in surrounding whitespace.

**6.7 Paired prediction vs. observation.** Stack the two as adjacent tracks with
identical x and color; let the alignment carry the comparison. Target regions
are translucent spans registered in the legend.

**6.8 Insets.** Connect a detail inset to its source region visibly — a bounding
box with connector lines, or a translucent wedge.

**6.9 Label the extremes.** On a scatter of named observations, direct-label at
least the maximum, minimum, and any flagged point with a thin leader line. After
rendering, verify every leader endpoint terminates within one marker radius of
the row it names.

---

## §7 Layout & narrative

**7.1 Show what is measured before the result.** A reader should grasp what's
being compared before seeing the comparison — via a plain-language title, a
labeled schematic, or panel ordering. Any schematic uses the same words and
glyphs as the data panels' labels.

**7.2 One figure, one message.** A multi-panel figure has a single sentence it
is trying to make true. Every panel either states it, supports it, or bounds it;
panels that do none of these belong in supplement.

**7.3 Legends live in whitespace.** Frameless, placed inside the figure's
natural whitespace, or replaced by direct labeling. Legend entries are
swatch-first, left-aligned, and resolve every visually distinct glyph on the
panel.

**7.4 Row-band headers for nested faceting.** When small multiples are grouped,
each group gets one spanning header, not repeated per-panel titles.

**7.5 The figure arc.** For a paper: Figure 1 renders the paper's one-sentence
pitch as data — scope, not architecture. Subsequent figures cover mechanism,
evidence, robustness, application. A panel is judged against the paper's pitch,
not just its own figure's claim; content moves between figures if that's where
the story needs it. (`paper-narrative` runs this review.)

**7.6 Don't re-decorate a passing panel.** Between revision rounds, a panel that
already passes is not made more visually complex to fix nothing. Adding marks or
labels to a clean panel is a regression.

---

## §8 Anti-patterns

These are correctness failures, not style preferences:

- Red and green as opposing categories.
- Filled bars on a log-scaled value axis.
- Colorbar ticks that are evenly spaced but miss the semantic center.
- A diverging colormap whose center is the data midpoint, not the semantic zero.
- An axis title that restates the tick labels.
- Explaining the direction of goodness only in the caption.
- A "reference" line drawn at a value that is itself one of the plotted points.
- An excluded row that enters a plotted summary statistic.
- A leader line whose nearest mark is not the row it labels.

---

## §9 Render-then-verify

After `fig.savefig(...)`, before `save_artifacts`:

**9.1 Geometric (bbox) check.**
```python
r = fig.canvas.get_renderer()
texts = [(t, t.get_window_extent(r)) for t in fig.findobj(mpl.text.Text)
         if t.get_text().strip() and t.get_visible()]
spines = [(s, s.get_window_extent(r)) for ax in fig.axes
          for s in ax.spines.values() if s.get_visible()]
ticklabels = {ax: set(ax.get_xticklabels(which='both') + ax.get_yticklabels(which='both'))
              for ax in fig.axes}
overlaps  = [(a, b) for i, (a, ba) in enumerate(texts) for b, bb in texts[i+1:] if ba.overlaps(bb)]
overlaps += [(t, s) for t, bt in texts for s, bs in spines
             if bt.overlaps(bs) and t not in ticklabels[s.axes]]
# assert: overlaps == [] and every text box lies within fig.bbox
```
Overlap is defined between *visible* boxes, and a tick label sitting on its own
spine is not a finding. Fix (move, shorten, stagger) and re-save until clean.

**9.2 Perceptual check.** The bbox check is geometric, not perceptual — it will
not catch a low-contrast label, a leader that crosses three others, or a series
color mistakable for another. Crop the saved PNG to each panel and look:
```python
fig.savefig("figure.png")
for letter, box in panel_crops(fig).items():
    host.view_image("figure.png", crop=box)
```
For each crop: Is every glyph and mark legible against its background? Does the
smallest plotted element have a stroke or stub? Do any leaders cross? Could any
series color be mistaken for another? Does the legend sit beside what it keys?
A perceptual defect that passes §9.1 is still a defect.

---
*When in doubt: fewer hues, more direct labels, raw data over summary stats, and
state what is being measured before showing the result.*
