input.code:
import json, numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, matplotlib as mpl
apply_figure_style(sizes=(8,7,6))

overlay=json.load(open("handoff/overlay.json"))
cpiv=pd.read_csv("handoff/cons_pivot.csv",index_col=0)
LIGS=["parent","N-Me_amide","alpha-Me-Phe","D-Phe","Reduced_amide","Thioamide","triazole"]
RECS=["CASR","HCAR1","NPY1R","MC4R","MRGPRD","TAS1R3","GPRC6A","TAS1R1","GPR171","OR51E1"]
cpiv=cpiv.reindex(LIGS)[RECS]
conf={"CASR":"H","HCAR1":"H","NPY1R":"H","MC4R":"H","MRGPRD":"H","TAS1R3":"L","GPRC6A":"M","TAS1R1":"M","GPR171":"M","OR51E1":"L"}

fig=plt.figure(figsize=(13,8.2))
gs=fig.add_gridspec(2,3,height_ratios=[1.05,1.0],hspace=0.42,wspace=0.32)

# ---- Panel A: consistency heatmap (spans top row) ----
axA=fig.add_subplot(gs[0,:])
data=cpiv.values.astype(float)
im=axA.imshow(data,cmap="viridis",vmin=0,vmax=1,aspect="auto")
axA.set_xticks(range(len(RECS)))
axA.set_xticklabels([f"{r}\n[{conf[r]}]" for r in RECS])
axA.set_yticks(range(len(LIGS)))
axA.set_yticklabels(LIGS)
for i in range(len(LIGS)):
    for j in range(len(RECS)):
        v=data[i,j]
        axA.text(j,i,f"{v:.2f}",ha="center",va="center",
                 color="white" if v<0.6 else "black",fontsize=6.2)
axA.set_title("Parent-contact consistency (0–1): does each analog reproduce the parent's binding mode & pharmacophore?",loc="left")
cb=fig.colorbar(im,ax=axA,fraction=0.025,pad=0.01); cb.set_label("consistency")
axA.text(1.005,-1.25,"pocket: [H]olo-PDB  [M]/[L] AlphaFold",transform=axA.transData,fontsize=6,color=META_GREY)
panel_letter(axA,'a')

# ---- Bottom row: pose overlays (PCA projection of pocket) ----
col={"parent":"#111111"}
palette=focal_palette(3) if 'focal_palette' in dir() else ["#d1495b","#3a7ca5"]
for k,(ax_i,t) in enumerate(zip([gs[1,0],gs[1,1],gs[1,2]],["HCAR1","MRGPRD","NPY1R"])):
    ax=fig.add_subplot(ax_i)
    od=overlay[t]; poses=od["poses"]; picks=od["picks"]
    # PCA plane from all pose atoms combined
    allc=np.vstack([np.array(v) for v in poses.values()])
    mu=allc.mean(0); U,S,Vt=np.linalg.svd(allc-mu); pc=Vt[:2]
    def proj(a): 
        a=np.array(a); return (a-mu)@pc.T
    # parent
    p=proj(poses["parent"]); ax.scatter(p[:,0],p[:,1],s=14,c="#111111",label="parent",zorder=3,edgecolors="none")
    cols=["#d1495b","#3a7ca5"]
    for lg,c in zip(picks,cols):
        q=proj(poses[lg]); ax.scatter(q[:,0],q[:,1],s=14,c=c,label=lg,alpha=0.75,zorder=2,edgecolors="none")
    # key residues
    for rn,(tag,xyz) in od["keyres"].items():
        r=proj([xyz])[0]
        ax.scatter(r[0],r[1],marker="*",s=150,c="#f0a500",edgecolors="black",linewidths=0.4,zorder=4)
        ax.annotate(rn,(r[0],r[1]),textcoords="offset points",xytext=(4,4),fontsize=6.5,fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_title(f"{t}: parent vs analog pose overlay",loc="left")
    ax.set_xlabel("pocket PC1 (Å)"); 
    if k==0: ax.set_ylabel("pocket PC2 (Å)")
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.legend(loc="best",fontsize=6,frameon=False)
    panel_letter(ax,chr(98+k))

fig.suptitle("Phase 2 — Lac-Phe analog differential docking: consistency-with-parent across candidate receptor panel",
             x=0.01,ha="left",fontsize=10,fontweight="bold")
fig.savefig("dock/docking_figures.png",dpi=200,bbox_inches="tight")
print("saved figure")

cb=fig.colorbar(im,ax=axA,fraction=0.025,pad=0.01); cb.set_label("consistency")

for k,(ax_i,t) in enumerate(zip([gs[1,0],gs[1,1],gs[1,2]],["HCAR1","MRGPRD","NPY1R"])):
    ax=fig.add_subplot(ax_i)
    od=overlay[t]; poses=od["poses"]; picks=od["picks"]
    allc=np.vstack([np.array(v) for v in poses.values()])
    mu=allc.mean(0); U,S,Vt=np.linalg.svd(allc-mu); pc=Vt[:2]
    proj=lambda a:(np.array(a)-mu)@pc.T
    p=proj(poses["parent"]); ax.scatter(p[:,0],p[:,1],s=15,c="#111111",label="parent",zorder=3,edgecolors="none")
    for lg,c in zip(picks,["#d1495b","#3a7ca5"]):
        q=proj(poses[lg]); ax.scatter(q[:,0],q[:,1],s=15,c=c,label=lg,alpha=0.72,zorder=2,edgecolors="none")
    for rn,(tag,xyz) in od["keyres"].items():
        r=proj([xyz])[0]
        ax.scatter(r[0],r[1],marker="*",s=160,c="#f0a500",edgecolors="black",linewidths=0.4,zorder=4)
        ax.annotate(rn,(r[0],r[1]),textcoords="offset points",xytext=(4,4),fontsize=6.5,fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_title(f"{t}: parent vs analog poses",loc="left")
    ax.set_xlabel("pocket PC1 (Å)")
    if k==0: ax.set_ylabel("pocket PC2 (Å)")
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.legend(loc="best",fontsize=6,frameon=False)
    ax.margins(0.12)
    panel_letter(ax,chr(98+k))

fig.suptitle("Phase 2 — Lac-Phe analog differential docking: consistency with parent across candidate receptor panel",
             x=0.01,ha="left",fontsize=10.5,fontweight="bold")
fig.savefig("dock/docking_figures.png",dpi=200,bbox_inches="tight")
print("saved")

input.code:
findings = """# Phase 2 — Candidate-Target Docking Screen for Lac-Phe Analogs

## Objective
The true Lac-Phe receptor is **unknown**. This screen is therefore **differential**, not
absolute: for each candidate pocket we ask whether each of the 6 hydrolysis-resistant analogs
reproduces the **parent Lac-Phe binding mode and key pharmacophore contacts**. Raw cross-chemotype
docking kcal/mol are **not** the ranking axis — parent-contact **consistency (0–1)** is.

## Method
- **Receptors (10):** 6 from experimental holo PDB structures, 4 from AlphaFold DB (v6).
  Box centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand where present (CASR·L-Trp, HCAR1·L-lactate,
  NPY1R·antagonist, MC4R·SHU9119, MRGPRD·β-alanine); fpocket-defined pocket for apo/AlphaFold targets.
- **Ligands (7):** parent + 6 analogs, 3D-embedded (ETKDGv3/MMFF), protonated at pH 7.4
  (carboxylate anionic, net ≈ −1; lactoyl-OH & amide-NH retained as H-bond donors).
- **Docking:** smina (AutoDock Vina 1.1.2 scoring), CPU-only, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses/pair, seed 42.
  70 runs (10×7), all completed.
- **Consistency score** (per analog, best of 9 poses): weighted blend of
  pocket overlap (centroid vs parent, 0.35) · contact-residue Tanimoto (0.30) ·
  carboxylate salt-bridge reproduced (0.20, when parent forms one) · phenyl π-stack reproduced (0.15).

## Key findings
1. **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout.** The parent docks into the L-lactate
   orthosteric pocket forming a **carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 and a π-stack to His256** — the
   same anchors lactate itself uses. **All 6 analogs reproduce both** (consistency 0.87–1.00),
   with D-Phe, thioamide, triazole and reduced-amide ≥0.98. This is the most mechanistically
   coherent hit: direct chemotype match (acyl-lactate) + high-confidence holo pocket + full
   pharmacophore reproduction across the whole analog series.
2. **MRGPRD (acyl/amino-acid sensor) is the second most consistent.** Parent anchors its
   carboxylate on Arg82; all analogs retain the salt bridge (consistency 0.89–0.97). β-alanine
   holo pocket = high confidence.
3. **Analog robustness ranking (mean consistency over high-confidence holo pockets):**
   D-Phe ≈ reduced-amide > thioamide ≈ α-Me-Phe > triazole ≈ N-Me-amide. D-Phe (stereochemistry-only
   change) is the most conservative, as expected. The **triazole** — the biggest scaffold change —
   is the most pocket-dependent: excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD/TAS1R3 but **diverges at NPY1R (0.69) and
   MC4R (0.76)** where it loses/relocates the phenyl π-stack.
4. **The carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore.** In every receptor where the parent forms
   a salt bridge (HCAR1, MRGPRD, GPRC6A, GPR171, OR51E1), analogs that keep the anionic carboxylate
   keep the salt bridge — consistent with Phase 1's decision to preserve it. Modifications are
   tolerated at the amide (N-Me, thioamide, reduced, triazole) as long as the pose keeps the
   COO⁻ oriented to the basic residue.

## Shortlist for Phase 3 (Boltz-2 co-folding confirmation)
Ranked by consistency × pocket-confidence × parent-pharmacophore richness (see shortlist_for_boltz.csv).
Top tier is **HCAR1** (D-Phe, thioamide, triazole, reduced-amide) then **MRGPRD** (triazole, D-Phe,
reduced-amide, N-Me) then **NPY1R** (N-Me, D-Phe). HCAR1 and MRGPRD are the two receptors worth
committing GPU budget to.

## Caveats
- **Docking scores are not affinities** and cross-chemotype kcal/mol are not comparable — used here
  only to rank poses within a pair, never across receptors.
- **AlphaFold pockets (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1) are low/low-moderate confidence.** Their
  boxes come from fpocket, not an experimental orthosteric ligand; high consistency there
  (e.g. GPRC6A 1.00, OR51E1 1.00) partly reflects small/shallow pockets that constrain all ligands
  similarly and should be treated cautiously. **TAS1R3 (9NOR) has no bound orthosteric ligand**
  (druggability 0.001) — its pocket is the weakest-defined of all; the reduced-amide's collapse
  there (0.09, different pocket) is likely pocket-definition noise, not signal.
- **Rigid-receptor docking** — no induced fit. Boltz-2 co-folding in Phase 3 addresses this.
- The screen cannot identify the true receptor; it identifies **which analogs behave most like the
  parent in each candidate pocket**, which is the correct de-risking question for a scaffold
  campaign against an unknown target.
"""
open("dock/phase2_findings.md","w").write(findings)
print("findings written", len(findings), "chars")

content.text:
Output submitted successfully. Phase 2 is complete.

## Phase 2 Summary — Candidate-Target Docking Screen

I ran a fully differential docking screen of all 7 Lac-Phe analogs against a 10-receptor candidate panel, with **parent-contact consistency** (not raw kcal/mol) as the ranking axis.

**Panel assembled (10 receptors):** I selected only ligand-plausible sensors/receptors from the prior candidate CSV — excluding effectors (KCNJ11/ABCC8) and transporters (SLC17A1/3). Six had experimental holo structures with boxes centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand (CASR/7DTV·L-Trp, **HCAR1/8Z8A·L-lactate**, NPY1R/5ZBQ, MC4R/6W25·SHU9119, MRGPRD/7Y12·β-alanine, TAS1R3/9NOR apo); four came from AlphaFold DB v6 with fpocket-defined boxes (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1), flagged as lower-confidence.

**Docking:** all 70 pairs (10×7) completed with smina, CPU-only, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses each.

**Key result — HCAR1 is the standout.** The parent docks into the lactate pocket forming a carboxylate salt bridge to **Arg94** and a π-stack to **His256** — the same anchors lactate uses — and all 6 analogs reproduce both (consistency 0.87–1.00). MRGPRD is a strong second (Arg82 salt bridge retained by every analog). Across the board, the anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore: wherever the parent salt-bridges, analogs keeping COO⁻ keep the bridge — validating Phase 1's decision to preserve it. D-Phe and the reduced amide are the most parent-faithful analogs; the triazole is the most pocket-dependent (excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD, diverges at NPY1R/MC4R).

**Shortlist for Phase 3:** HCAR1 (D-Phe, thioamide, triazole, reduced-amide) and MRGPRD (triazole, D-Phe, reduced-amide, N-Me) are the pairs worth committing Boltz-2 GPU budget to.

![Phase 2 differential docking — consistency heatmap and pose overlays]({{artifact:7f3171ad-92f5-432e-986f-d6742408deec}})

**Deliverables:**
- [parent_contact_consistency.csv]({{artifact:1ca687d9-4294-4626-b073-73cd56915914}}) — primary deliverable: consistency 0–1 per (analog, target) with contacts reproduced and notes
- [shortlist_for_boltz.csv]({{artifact:28ac45c4-b566-4102-b928-0d955d8a5a0a}}) — top 10 pairs for Phase 3
- [receptor_panel.csv]({{artifact:de909ade-136b-491e-92fb-4bcb859818a5}}) — panel with structure source and pocket confidence
- [docking_scores.csv]({{artifact:1f08cc59-b393-436a-ac3b-76b268f20ac7}}) — raw smina scores (for within-pair pose ranking only)
- [docking_figures.png]({{artifact:7f3171ad-92f5-432e-986f-d6742408deec}}) — consistency heatmap + pose overlays
- [phase2_findings.md]({{artifact:91298484-bea5-40a5-8c13-961632406d60}}) — narrative summary with caveats

**Caveats carried forward:** the true receptor remains unknown (this ranks analog-vs-parent behavior per pocket, not receptor identity); cross-chemotype docking scores are not comparable; AlphaFold and apo pockets (esp. TAS1R3, druggability 0.001) are low-confidence; and rigid docking has no induced fit — which Phase 3 Boltz-2 co-folding addresses.

[Auditor] <verification_findings starts="0,90,119">
A fresh-context reviewer traced your work and found 2 issues:

  1. [fail] "phase2_findings.md claims 'All 6 analogs reproduce both' (salt-bridge to Arg94 AND π-stack to His256) at HCAR1"
     The saved parent_contact_consistency.csv (artifact 1ca687d9-4294-4626-b073-73cd56915914) row for analog=N-Me_amide, target=HCAR1 shows saltbridge=1.0 but pistack=0.4 with pi_partner=('TYR','263','A') and notes='π-stack to different arom' — i.e. N-Me_amide does NOT reproduce the parent's π-stack to His256, it stacks against a different residue (Tyr263). This directly contradicts the findings.md headline claim that all 6 analogs reproduce both the salt bridge and the π-stack at HCAR1.

c=pd.read_csv("dock/parent_contact_consistency.csv")
# HCAR1 pi-stack reproduction per analog
h=c[c.target=="HCAR1"][["analog","saltbridge","pistack","pi_partner","consistency"]]
print("=== HCAR1 ===")

# high-confidence pockets
high=["CASR","HCAR1","NPY1R","MC4R","MRGPRD"]
m=(c[c.target.isin(high) & (c.analog!="parent")]
   .groupby("analog").consistency.mean().sort_values(ascending=False).round(3))
print("\n=== mean consistency across 5 high-conf pockets ===")
print(m.to_string())

Both Phase 1 tracks are in. The picture that emerges is a genuine three-way tension:

- **Triazole** — best combined pharmacophore fidelity + fully hydrolysis-inert, **but** a predicted DILI (hepatotoxicity) flag of 0.741, ~2× every other analog.
- **Thioamide** — most shape-conservative isostere (key groups within ~0.5 Å of parent), clean ADMET, but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
- **Reduced amide** — hydrolysis-inert and cleanest ADMET, but worst pharmacophore match (4.3 Å) and a new basic amine.
- **α-Me-Phe** — best raw 3D overlay but flagged: α-H methylation is a known affinity-killer for amino-acid receptors, so the overlay is misleading.
- ChEMBL target prediction saw Lac-Phe as a **cleavable dipeptide** (peptidase/transporter neighbors, no GPCR signal) — which reinforces the hydrolysis premise rather than corroborating a GPCR, so Phase 2 rightly leans on the curated biology-driven panel.

Let me record progress and launch Phase 2 docking.

# Boltz-2 on a local Apple-Silicon GPU

Runs the same Boltz-2 co-folding + affinity workflow you'd run on a cloud A100,
but on an M-series Mac's GPU — no per-hour cloud cost.

## The one hard constraint

**The Claude Science sandbox cannot reach the Mac's Metal GPU.**
`MTLCreateSystemDefaultDevice()` returns NULL and `torch.backends.mps.is_available()`
is `False` inside these kernels — verified. So the GPU step **cannot** run in a
`python`/`bash` cell here. It must run in the user's **own Terminal**, where Metal
is live. This skill therefore splits the work:

1. **In Claude Science (CPU-safe):** build Boltz YAML inputs + `run_local.sh`.
2. **User, in Terminal (uses the GPU):** `bash setup_local_boltz.sh` once, then
   `conda activate boltz-mps && bash run_local.sh`.
3. **Back in Claude Science:** parse the `out/` folder into a table + structures.

Do not try to "just run boltz on the GPU" from a cell — it will silently run on
CPU or fail to find a device. Always produce the bundle and hand off.

## Helper functions (auto-loaded from kernel.py)

- `boltz_yaml(protein_seq, ligand_smiles, affinity=True, msa=None)` → YAML string
  for one complex. `msa=None` omits the msa line (pair with `--use_msa_server`);
  `msa="empty"` = single-sequence mode; `msa="<path>"` reuses a cached `.a3m`.
- `prepare_boltz_run(pairs, receptors, out_dir="boltz_local", msa_mode="server",
  recycling_steps=3, diffusion_samples=3)` → writes the full run bundle. `pairs`
  is a list of `{name, ligand, smiles, receptor}`; `receptors` maps
  `receptor_key -> protein_sequence`. `msa_mode="server"` generates one MSA per
  receptor via the Boltz MSA server and reuses it (best quality, needs network
  once); `"empty"` is single-sequence, fully offline.
- `collect_boltz_results(out_root)` → `(df, structures)`. `df` has
  `affinity_pred_value`, `affinity_probability_binary`, `ptm`, `iptm`,
  `complex_plddt` per complex; `structures` maps name -> model .pdb/.cif paths.

## Workflow

### 1. Build the bundle (in a `python` cell)
```python
receptors = {"5HT2A": "SLLHLQEK...QYKE"}   # receptor protein sequence(s)
pairs = [
    {"name":"DiPT_5HT2A","ligand":"DiPT","smiles":"CC(C)N(C(C)C)CCc1c[nH]c2ccccc12","receptor":"5HT2A"},
    {"name":"DMT_5HT2A", "ligand":"DMT", "smiles":"CN(C)CCc1c[nH]c2ccccc12","receptor":"5HT2A"},
]
bundle = prepare_boltz_run(pairs, receptors, out_dir="boltz_local", msa_mode="server")
```
Then `save_artifacts` the whole bundle (tar it: `tar czf boltz_local.tar.gz boltz_local`)
so the user can download it.

### 2. Hand off to the user (put this in your reply)
Tell the user to, in Terminal, in the unpacked bundle folder:
```
bash setup_local_boltz.sh          # once: creates conda env boltz-mps + installs Boltz w/ MPS
conda activate boltz-mps
bash run_local.sh                  # runs every complex on the GPU, writes out/
```

### 3. Parse results (after they return the out/ folder)
Have them upload/point to `out/`, then:
```python
df, structures = collect_boltz_results("out")
```
Save `df` as a CSV and the structure `.pdb`/`.cif` as artifacts.

## Settings & expectations

- Defaults `--recycling_steps 3 --diffusion_samples 3 --no_kernels` mirror the
  standard cloud run. `--no_kernels` disables the CUDA-only fused kernels (required
  off-CUDA). `--accelerator mps` selects the Metal GPU; MPS support in Boltz comes
  from an open PR (jwohlwend/boltz PR #527), checked out by number in
  `setup_local_boltz.sh` — confirm it still applies cleanly if Boltz has moved on.
- `PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1` is set so any op without a Metal kernel falls
  back to CPU instead of erroring. Expect it to be slower than an A100 and for a
  few ops to run on CPU; for a handful of complexes it's an easy run, for a
  ~100-compound benchmark budget several hours to overnight.
- MSA server is used once per receptor then cached i

# Publication-Grade Figure Rules

*A checklist for correct, legible, internally-consistent scientific figures. This
skill does not impose a visual house style — frame, font, and palette are
parameters. Once loaded, call `apply_figure_style()` before plotting.*

## §0 Scope

**Load trigger** (same rule as the system prompt's "Publication-grade plots"
section): this skill is for **final-deliverable figures** — those shipping in a
report, paper, or export, or saved as an artifact the user will keep — not for
exploratory/intermediate plots (quick looks, EDA, sanity checks), which are
drawn plainly without it. Once loaded, "every plot" below means every plot you
render toward the deliverable.

§1–§3, §8, and §9 are **correctness** — they apply to every plot, in every
context, and have no aesthetic content. §4–§7 are **guidance** — defaults that
produce a clean result but that a deliberate alternative can override
(individual rules inside §4–§7 that state a factual/perceptual invariant — e.g.
§4.4 semantic-zero centering, §4.5 CVD, §6.9 leader anchoring — still bind). On
its own, this skill is the inner tier (make one plot good); `figure-composer`
and `paper-narrative` supply multi-panel and whole-paper context.

---

## §1 Data fidelity & self-consistency

**1.1 Excluded rows.** A row marked excluded or flagged in the source data is
either omitted entirely or drawn with a visually distinct open/hatched marker
and named in the key. It **never** enters a summary statistic plotted alongside
the included rows.

**1.2 Comparable conditions only.** Arms measured under non-comparable
conditions (different N, epoch budget, initialization, protocol) are not plotted
as visual peers. Separate them with a facet break or a marker on the label, and
state the difference once in the caption.

**1.3 Self-consistency.** Every key, threshold, and title inside the figure must
be satisfied by every plotted row. Before saving, walk each categorical outcome
label back to the rule that defines it; if a row's value contradicts its label
or the title, the figure is wrong, not the data.

**1.4 Claim-titles must be true.** A sentence-title (§5.1) is tested against
every category on the axis before rendering. If any contradicts it, qualify the
title ("on 3 of 4 pairs") or downgrade it to a description.

**1.5 State n and what was held fixed.** Every panel that draws a summary mark
states `n` and the unit of replication, and every small-multiple that holds a
variable fixed states the fixed value — in the panel or, when §2 budget is
tight, in the caption.

**1.6 Reference structure is reference.** A tree, ordering, or topology drawn as
*context* (a scale bar, a category strip) uses an established reference, not
one inferred from the plotted data. Infer the structure only when the structure
*is* the result.

**1.7 One number per claim.** A quantitative claim (runtime, accuracy, count)
has exactly one canonical value across every panel, caption, and the abstract.
Define what it measures and use that value everywhere.

---

## §2 Label economy — floor and ceiling

The figure shows the pattern; the **caption** carries the context. Design for a
general scientific reader, not the author.

**2.1 Floor (non-removable).** Every distinct mark, series, glyph, or comparator
must be identifiable from the figure alone. The caption explains *why it
matters*, not *what it is*. A label is non-removable if deleting it leaves a
reader asking "what is that?"; it is removable only if the question becomes "why
is that there?". Comparator labels name the thing ("prior method", "no joint
training"), never a bare role word ("baseline", "previous"). Any term a general
scientist can't parse gets a one-word gloss.

**2.2 Ceiling.** Per panel: title + axis labels + tick labels + series identity
(labeled once per row of small multiples) + at most 2–3 result annotations.

> Here's a computational testing plan for the seven Lac-Phe analogs. It's organized so each stage gates the next — you don't dock everything if it fails stability triage, and you don't invest in MD until docking discriminates.

> ## Stage 1 — Confirm the design premise: hydrolytic stability
> The whole point is amide-bond resistance, so test that first, in silico.
> - **Bond-cleavage energetics.** For the parent amide vs. thioamide, reduced-amide, and triazole, compute the relative activation barrier / thermodynamics of hydrolysis with a QM approach (e.g., DFT ωB97X-D/6-31+G* with an implicit water/PCM model, optionally a tetrahedral-intermediate transition-state scan). Confirms the isosteres are genuinely higher-barrier, not just intuition.
> - **Enzymatic-liability prediction.** Run the panel through a protease/amidase susceptibility predictor and a general metabolic-site predictor (e.g., a SoM/FAME-type model, or a CYP site-of-metabolism tool) to flag *any* remaining soft spots — the triazole
> …[1941 chars elided]…
>  the amide-retaining ("safe on activity, weaker on stability") vs. isostere ("stable, higher activity-loss risk") tension is explicit rather than argued.
> 
> **Realistic caveats to build into the plan up front:** the unresolved receptor is the dominant uncertainty — a beautiful docking result against a wrong target is worthless, so keep Stage 2 assumptions labeled and, ideally, run more than one candidate target. Absolute docking scores don't rank reliably across chemotypes (amide vs. triazole vs. thioamide differ enough that you should lean on FEP/MD, not raw docking scores). And none of this substitutes for the eventual in vitro anorectic/receptor assay.

Both Phase 1 tracks are in. The picture that emerges is a genuine three-way tension:

- **Triazole** — best combined pharmacophore fidelity + fully hydrolysis-inert, **but** a predicted DILI (hepatotoxicity) flag of 0.741, ~2× every other analog.
- **Thioamide** — most shape-conservative isostere (key groups within ~0.5 Å of parent), clean ADMET, but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
- **Reduced amide** — hydrolysis-inert and cleanest ADMET, but worst pharmacophore match (4.3 Å) and a new basic amine.
- **α-Me-Phe** — best raw 3D overlay but flagged: α-H methylation is a known affinity-killer for amino-acid receptors, so the overlay is misleading.
- ChEMBL target prediction saw Lac-Phe as a **cleavable dipeptide** (peptidase/transporter neighbors, no GPCR signal) — which reinforces the hydrolysis premise rather than corroborating a GPCR, so Phase 2 rightly leans on the curated biology-driven panel.

Let me record progress and launch Phase 2 docking.

# Boltz-2 on a local Apple-Silicon GPU

Runs the same Boltz-2 co-folding + affinity workflow you'd run on a cloud A100,
but on an M-series Mac's GPU — no per-hour cloud cost.

## The one hard constraint

**The Claude Science sandbox cannot reach the Mac's Metal GPU.**
`MTLCreateSystemDefaultDevice()` returns NULL and `torch.backends.mps.is_available()`
is `False` inside these kernels — verified. So the GPU step **cannot** run in a
`python`/`bash` cell here. It must run in the user's **own Terminal**, where Metal
is live. This skill therefore splits the work:

1. **In Claude Science (CPU-safe):** build Boltz YAML inputs + `run_local.sh`.
2. **User, in Terminal (uses the GPU):** `bash setup_local_boltz.sh` once, then
   `conda activate boltz-mps && bash run_local.sh`.
3. **Back in Claude Science:** parse the `out/` folder into a table + structures.

Do not try to "just run boltz on the GPU" from a cell — it will silently run on
CPU or fail to find a device. Always produce the bundle and hand off.

## Helper functions (auto-loaded from kernel.py)

- `boltz_yaml(protein_seq, ligand_smiles, affinity=True, msa=None)` → YAML string
  for one complex. `msa=None` omits the msa line (pair with `--use_msa_server`);
  `msa="empty"` = single-sequence mode; `msa="<path>"` reuses a cached `.a3m`.
- `prepare_boltz_run(pairs, receptors, out_dir="boltz_local", msa_mode="server",
  recycling_steps=3, diffusion_samples=3)` → writes the full run bundle. `pairs`
  is a list of `{name, ligand, smiles, receptor}`; `receptors` maps
  `receptor_key -> protein_sequence`. `msa_mode="server"` generates one MSA per
  receptor via the Boltz MSA server and reuses it (best quality, needs network
  once); `"empty"` is single-sequence, fully offline.
- `collect_boltz_results(out_root)` → `(df, structures)`. `df` has
  `affinity_pred_value`, `affinity_probability_binary`, `ptm`, `iptm`,
  `complex_plddt` per complex; `structures` maps name -> model .pdb/.cif paths.

## Workflow

### 1. Build the bundle (in a `python` cell)
```python
receptors = {"5HT2A": "SLLHLQEK...QYKE"}   # receptor protein sequence(s)
pairs = [
    {"name":"DiPT_5HT2A","ligand":"DiPT","smiles":"CC(C)N(C(C)C)CCc1c[nH]c2ccccc12","receptor":"5HT2A"},
    {"name":"DMT_5HT2A", "ligand":"DMT", "smiles":"CN(C)CCc1c[nH]c2ccccc12","receptor":"5HT2A"},
]
bundle = prepare_boltz_run(pairs, receptors, out_dir="boltz_local", msa_mode="server")
```
Then `save_artifacts` the whole bundle (tar it: `tar czf boltz_local.tar.gz boltz_local`)
so the user can download it.

### 2. Hand off to the user (put this in your reply)
Tell the user to, in Terminal, in the unpacked bundle folder:
```
bash setup_local_boltz.sh          # once: creates conda env boltz-mps + installs Boltz w/ MPS
conda activate boltz-mps
bash run_local.sh                  # runs every complex on the GPU, writes out/
```

### 3. Parse results (after they return the out/ folder)
Have them upload/point to `out/`, then:
```python
df, structures = collect_boltz_results("out")
```
Save `df` as a CSV and the structure `.pdb`/`.cif` as artifacts.

## Settings & expectations

- Defaults `--recycling_steps 3 --diffusion_samples 3 --no_kernels` mirror the
  standard cloud run. `--no_kernels` disables the CUDA-only fused kernels (required
  off-CUDA). `--accelerator mps` selects the Metal GPU; MPS support in Boltz comes
  from an open PR (jwohlwend/boltz PR #527), checked out by number in
  `setup_local_boltz.sh` — confirm it still applies cleanly if Boltz has moved on.
- `PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1` is set so any op without a Metal kernel falls
  back to CPU instead of erroring. Expect it to be slower than an A100 and for a
  few ops to run on CPU; for a handful of complexes it's an easy run, for a
  ~100-compound benchmark budget several hours to overnight.
- MSA server is used once per receptor then cached i

# Publication-Grade Figure Rules

*A checklist for correct, legible, internally-consistent scientific figures. This
skill does not impose a visual house style — frame, font, and palette are
parameters. Once loaded, call `apply_figure_style()` before plotting.*

## §0 Scope

**Load trigger** (same rule as the system prompt's "Publication-grade plots"
section): this skill is for **final-deliverable figures** — those shipping in a
report, paper, or export, or saved as an artifact the user will keep — not for
exploratory/intermediate plots (quick looks, EDA, sanity checks), which are
drawn plainly without it. Once loaded, "every plot" below means every plot you
render toward the deliverable.

§1–§3, §8, and §9 are **correctness** — they apply to every plot, in every
context, and have no aesthetic content. §4–§7 are **guidance** — defaults that
produce a clean result but that a deliberate alternative can override
(individual rules inside §4–§7 that state a factual/perceptual invariant — e.g.
§4.4 semantic-zero centering, §4.5 CVD, §6.9 leader anchoring — still bind). On
its own, this skill is the inner tier (make one plot good); `figure-composer`
and `paper-narrative` supply multi-panel and whole-paper context.

---

## §1 Data fidelity & self-consistency

**1.1 Excluded rows.** A row marked excluded or flagged in the source data is
either omitted entirely or drawn with a visually distinct open/hatched marker
and named in the key. It **never** enters a summary statistic plotted alongside
the included rows.

**1.2 Comparable conditions only.** Arms measured under non-comparable
conditions (different N, epoch budget, initialization, protocol) are not plotted
as visual peers. Separate them with a facet break or a marker on the label, and
state the difference once in the caption.

**1.3 Self-consistency.** Every key, threshold, and title inside the figure must
be satisfied by every plotted row. Before saving, walk each categorical outcome
label back to the rule that defines it; if a row's value contradicts its label
or the title, the figure is wrong, not the data.

**1.4 Claim-titles must be true.** A sentence-title (§5.1) is tested against
every category on the axis before rendering. If any contradicts it, qualify the
title ("on 3 of 4 pairs") or downgrade it to a description.

**1.5 State n and what was held fixed.** Every panel that draws a summary mark
states `n` and the unit of replication, and every small-multiple that holds a
variable fixed states the fixed value — in the panel or, when §2 budget is
tight, in the caption.

**1.6 Reference structure is reference.** A tree, ordering, or topology drawn as
*context* (a scale bar, a category strip) uses an established reference, not
one inferred from the plotted data. Infer the structure only when the structure
*is* the result.

**1.7 One number per claim.** A quantitative claim (runtime, accuracy, count)
has exactly one canonical value across every panel, caption, and the abstract.
Define what it measures and use that value everywhere.

---

## §2 Label economy — floor and ceiling

The figure shows the pattern; the **caption** carries the context. Design for a
general scientific reader, not the author.

**2.1 Floor (non-removable).** Every distinct mark, series, glyph, or comparator
must be identifiable from the figure alone. The caption explains *why it
matters*, not *what it is*. A label is non-removable if deleting it leaves a
reader asking "what is that?"; it is removable only if the question becomes "why
is that there?". Comparator labels name the thing ("prior method", "no joint
training"), never a bare role word ("baseline", "previous"). Any term a general
scientist can't parse gets a one-word gloss.

**2.2 Ceiling.** Per panel: title + axis labels + tick labels + series identity
(labeled once per row of small multiples) + at most 2–3 result annotations.

{"filename":"Lac-Phe_analog_report.md","content_type":"text/markdown","size_bytes":9134,"content":"# A hydrolysis-resistant Lac-Phe analog: computational campaign report

**Objective.** Design and computationally triage an analog of **Lac-Phe** (N-lactoyl-L-phenylalanine),
the exercise-induced appetite-suppressing metabolite, that resists hydrolysis of its central amide bond
while retaining the parent's activity-relevant pharmacophore. All work was done on a laptop (14 CPU cores,
no discrete GPU beyond a small Apple-Silicon budget reserved for co-folding); the true receptor is **unknown**.

---

## Executive summary

Seven structures were carried through a five-stage funnel: the parent plus six analogs spanning
**amide-retaining** (N-methyl, α-methyl-Phe, D-Phe) and **backbone-replacement** (reduced amide,
thioamide, 1,2,3-triazole) strategies. Everything decisive was computed on CPU with no target
assumption; the scarce GPU budget is reserved for confirming survivors.

**Lead recommendation: the 1,2,3-triazole isostere** — it is the only design that makes amide
hydrolysis structurally impossible while preserving the parent's shape and the load-bearing anionic
carboxylate, and it docks consistently into the top candidate pocket. Its one liability is a predicted
hepatotoxicity (DILI) flag that must be checked experimentally.

**Backups: reduced amide** (also hydrolysis-inert, cleanest ADMET, but a weaker pharmacophore match and
a new basic amine) and **thioamide** (most shape-conservative isostere, clean ADMET, but only partially
stabilized — it still bears a hydrolyzable C=S).

**α-Methyl-Phe is explicitly de-prioritized** despite the best raw 3D overlay: α-hydrogen methylation is
a known affinity-killer at amino-acid/taste-type receptors, so its overlay is a geometric artifact.

---

## The design problem

Lac-Phe is an *N*-lactoyl amino acid; the hydrolysable liability is the single **amide bond** joining the
lactoyl and phenylalanine halves. The same enzymology that produces and clears it (CNDP2 — which
synthesizes it reversibly — plus serum peptidases) reads that bond as a cleavable peptide linkage. Every
design protects that one bond, by one of two routes: **block enzyme recognition** while keeping the
carbonyl (cheaper, closer to parent, but still in-principle cleavable) or **remove the carbonyl entirely**
(non-hydrolysable by class, at the cost of larger pharmacophore change).

---

## Stage 1 — Hydrolytic stability (GFN2-xTB, CPU)

Semi-empirical QM (implicit water) on the scissile linkage confirmed the design premise:

- **Carbonyl-free isosteres are genuinely inert.** The **1,2,3-triazole** (aromatic heterocycle, no
  electrophilic carbon; scissile C–N Wiberg bond order ≈ 0.98) and the **reduced amide** (sp³ C–N, BO ≈ 1.01)
  have no carbonyl for water or a peptidase to attack — non-hydrolysable by class.
- **Thioamide** is modestly stabilized (highest scissile C–N bond order 1.39, least-electrophilic scissile
  carbon) but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
- **N-methylation** is only a marginal fix; **D-Phe** relies purely on stereochemistry and leaves the amide
  intact.

*Caveat:* these are ground-state proxies (bond order, atomic charge), not computed hydrolysis activation
free energies; the neutral acid form was modeled while the physiological species is the carboxylate anion.

## Stage 1 — Pharmacophore fidelity (RDKit O3A, CPU)

Each analog was O3A-aligned to the parent's low-energy conformer and scored on preservation of the three
SAR-relevant groups (terminal carboxylate, lactoyl-OH, amide H-bond vectors):

- **α-Me-Phe** has the best raw overlay (carboxylate/lactoyl-OH within ~0.3 Å) but is **flagged as a
  recognition risk** — see summary.
- **Triazole** preserves shape well (shape Tanimoto 0.60) though the lactoyl-OH swings ~3.9 Å on the new linker.
- **Reduced amide** is the weakest pharmacophore match (mean key-group deviation ~4.3 Å) and adds a basic amine.

## Stage 2 — ADMET + synthetic accessibility (CPU)

ADMET-AI (104 endpoints) gave uniform, clean hERG/CYP/AMES safety and 49–64% plasma-protein binding across
all seven. Two signals mattered:

- **The triazole carries a predicted DILI probability of 0.74** (above threshold, ~2× every other analog;
  reduced amide is cleanest at 0.14). This is the single differentiating ADMET liability and gates the lead.
- **Shared low passive permeability** (Caco-2 logP_app −5.2 to −5.7) is intrinsic to the polar zwitterion and
  **not rescued by any modification** — a scaffold-level property, not an analog discriminator.

All seven are synthetically easy (Ertl SAscore 2.4–3.1); the triazole is made by CuAAC, the reduced amide by
reductive amination. *Caveat:* the clearance/half-life regressors returned physically impossible values
(out-of-domain for endogenous-metabolite zwitterions) and were excluded from ranking.

**Ligand-based target prediction** (ChEMBL similarity) saw Lac-Phe as a **cleavable dipeptide** — its nearest
bioactive neighbors annotate to peptidases/transporters (Calpain-1, PepT2/SLC15A2), not GPCRs. This does not
corroborate a specific receptor; it reinforces the hydrolysis-liability premise and is why Stage 2 docking
relies on a biology-driven candidate panel rather than a similarity-derived one.

## Stage 2 — Candidate-target docking (smina, CPU, target-unknown)

Because the receptor is unknown, docking was run against a **curated 10-receptor candidate panel** (6
experimental holo pockets + 4 AlphaFold models) and scored on **parent-contact consistency (0–1)** — does
each analog reproduce the *parent's* binding mode and key contacts — **not** raw cross-chemotype kcal/mol.

- **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout, mechanistically coherent hit.** Lac-Phe is an
  acyl-lactate; the parent docks into the L-lactate orthosteric pocket with a carboxylate salt bridge to
  **Arg94** and a π-stack to **His256**. All six analogs reproduce the salt bridge; five of six also keep the
  π-stack (consistency 0.87–1.00).
- **MRGPRD** (β-alanine sensor, holo) is second — all analogs retain the Arg82 salt bridge.
- **The anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore** in every salt-bridging pocket — validating
  the decision to preserve it. The amide position tolerates modification as long as the pose keeps COO⁻
  oriented to the basic residue.
- Analog faithfulness (mean over 5 holo pockets): D-Phe ≈ reduced-amide > thioamide > N-Me > α-Me-Phe ≈
  triazole. The triazole is the most pocket-dependent (excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD, diverges at NPY1R/MC4R).

*Caveats:* docking scores are not affinities; AlphaFold pockets are low-confidence; rigid-receptor docking has
no induced fit (Stage 3 addresses this). This ranks analog behavior relative to the parent — it does **not**
identify the true receptor.

## Stage 3 — Physics-based confirmation (Boltz-2, local Apple-Silicon GPU)

Co-fold + affinity-head inputs were prepared for the **survivors** (parent + triazole, reduced amide,
thioamide, D-Phe) against the **two lead receptors (HCAR1, MRGPRD)** — 10 complexes × 3 diffusion samples,
inside the ~100-run/12-h budget. The Claude Science sandbox cannot reach the Mac's Metal GPU, so this stage
is **handed off to the user** to run locally; affinity scores will be parsed back and folded into the matrix.

---

## Stage 4 — Ranked decision matrix

Composite = 0.45·hydrolytic-stability + 0.40·activity-retention + 0.15·ADMET-factor, where activity-retention
blends pharmacophore fidelity and docking consistency and applies the α-Me-Phe recognition penalty.

| Rank | Analog | Composite | Stability | Activity retention | DILI | SAscore | Route |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Lac-Phe (parent) | 0.59 | 0.10 | 1.00 | 0.32 | 2.44 | amide coupling |
| **1** | **1,2,3-triazole** | **0.87** | 1.00 | 0.80 | 0.74 ⚠ | 3.05 | CuAAC |
| 2 | Reduced amide | 0.80 | 0.95 | 0.56 | 0.14 | 2.52 | reductive amination |
| 3 | Thioamide | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.71 | 0.36 | 2.88 | Lawesson / thioacylation |
| 4 | N-Me amide | 0.55 | 0.35 | 0.63 | 0.42 | 2.81 | amide coupling (N-Me-Phe) |
| 5 | D-Phe | 0.54 | 0.15 | 0.82 | 0.35 | 2.44 | amide coupling (D-Phe) |
| 6 | α-Me-Phe | 0.45 | 0.35 | 0.37† | 0.38 | 2.87 | amide coupling (α-Me-Phe) |

⚠ triazole DILI flag is the one liability to verify. † α-Me-Phe activity-retention includes the
literature-flagged recognition penalty.

---

## Overall caveats

1. **The true Lac-Phe receptor is unknown.** HCAR1 is a mechanistically attractive, docking-consistent
   *hypothesis* (acyl-lactate binding the lactate receptor), not an established target. All target-dependent
   conclusions are conditional on it.
2. **Docking ranks consistency-with-parent, not affinity**, and cannot prove a target.
3. **This is computational prioritization**, not validation. The decisive experiments are wet-lab: hydrolytic
   half-life (serum/CNDP2), a receptor/β-arrestin assay once a target is confirmed, and the in vivo anorectic
   readout. Any modification risks losing activity — the amide-retaining vs. isostere axis is exactly the
   stability-vs-activity trade-off that only assays can resolve.
"}

[tool_result for=toolu_019rhykWsVj6GFxdiq31EURj] {"filename":"phase2_findings.md","content_type":"text/markdown","size_bytes":4756,"content":"# Phase 2 — Candidate-Target Docking Screen for Lac-Phe Analogs

## Objective
The true Lac-Phe receptor is **unknown**. This screen is therefore **differential**, not
absolute: for each candidate pocket we ask whether each of the 6 hydrolysis-resistant analogs
reproduces the **parent Lac-Phe binding mode and key pharmacophore contacts**. Raw cross-chemotype
docking kcal/mol are **not** the ranking axis — parent-contact **consistency (0–1)** is.

## Method
- **Receptors (10):** 6 from experimental holo PDB structures, 4 from AlphaFold DB (v6).
  Box centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand where present (CASR·L-Trp, HCAR1·L-lactate,
  NPY1R·antagonist, MC4R·SHU9119, MRGPRD·β-alanine); fpocket-defined pocket for apo/AlphaFold targets.
- **Ligands (7):** parent + 6 analogs, 3D-embedded (ETKDGv3/MMFF), protonated at pH 7.4
  (carboxylate anionic, net ≈ −1; lactoyl-OH & amide-NH retained as H-bond donors).
- **Docking:** smina (AutoDock Vina 1.1.2 scoring), CPU-only, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses/pair, seed 42.
  70 runs (10×7), all completed.
- **Consistency score** (per analog, best of 9 poses): weighted blend of
  pocket overlap (centroid vs parent, 0.35) · contact-residue Tanimoto (0.30) ·
  carboxylate salt-bridge reproduced (0.20, when parent forms one) · phenyl π-stack reproduced (0.15).

## Key findings
1. **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout.** The parent docks into the L-lactate
   orthosteric pocket forming a **carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 and a π-stack to His256** — the
   same anchors lactate itself uses. **All 6 analogs reproduce the salt bridge; 5 of 6 also
   reproduce the His256 π-stack** (consistency 0.87–1.00, with D-Phe, thioamide, triazole and
   reduced-amide ≥0.98). The exception is **N-Me-amide**, which keeps the Arg94 salt bridge but
   relocates its phenyl to a different aromatic (Tyr263) rather than His256. This is still the most
   mechanistically coherent hit: direct chemotype match (acyl-lactate) + high-confidence holo
   pocket + near-complete pharmacophore reproduction across the analog series.
2. **MRGPRD (acyl/amino-acid sensor) is the second most consistent.** Parent anchors its
   carboxylate on Arg82; all analogs retain the salt bridge (consistency 0.89–0.97). β-alanine
   holo pocket = high confidence.
3. **Analog robustness ranking (mean consistency over the 5 high-confidence holo pockets
   CASR/HCAR1/NPY1R/MC4R/MRGPRD):** D-Phe (0.944) ≈ reduced-amide (0.943) > thioamide (0.933) >
   N-Me-amide (0.888) > α-Me-Phe (0.873) ≈ triazole (0.867). D-Phe (stereochemistry-only change)
   is the most conservative, as expected. The **triazole** — the biggest scaffold change — is the
   most pocket-dependent: excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD/TAS1R3 but **diverges at NPY1R (0.69) and
   MC4R (0.76)** where it loses/relocates the phenyl π-stack.
4. **The carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore.** In every receptor where the parent forms
   a salt bridge (HCAR1, MRGPRD, GPRC6A, GPR171, OR51E1), analogs that keep the anionic carboxylate
   keep the salt bridge — consistent with Phase 1's decision to preserve it. Modifications are
   tolerated at the amide (N-Me, thioamide, reduced, triazole) as long as the pose keeps the
   COO⁻ oriented to the basic residue.

## Shortlist for Phase 3 (Boltz-2 co-folding confirmation)
Ranked by consistency × pocket-confidence × parent-pharmacophore richness (see shortlist_for_boltz.csv).
Top tier is **HCAR1** (D-Phe, thioamide, triazole, reduced-amide) then **MRGPRD** (triazole, D-Phe,
reduced-amide, N-Me) then **NPY1R** (N-Me, D-Phe). HCAR1 and MRGPRD are the two receptors worth
committing GPU budget to.

## Caveats
- **Docking scores are not affinities** and cross-chemotype kcal/mol are not comparable — used here
  only to rank poses within a pair, never across receptors.
- **AlphaFold pockets (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1) are low/low-moderate confidence.** Their
  boxes come from fpocket, not an experimental orthosteric ligand; high consistency there
  (e.g. GPRC6A 1.00, OR51E1 1.00) partly reflects small/shallow pockets that constrain all ligands
  similarly and should be treated cautiously. **TAS1R3 (9NOR) has no bound orthosteric ligand**
  (druggability 0.001) — its pocket is the weakest-defined of all; the reduced-amide's collapse
  there (0.09, different pocket) is likely pocket-definition noise, not signal.
- **Rigid-receptor docking** — no induced fit. Boltz-2 co-folding in Phase 3 addresses this.
- The screen cannot identify the true receptor; it identifies **which analogs behave most like the
  parent in each candidate pocket**, which is the correct de-risking question for a scaffold
  campaign against an unknown target.
"}

**Objective.** Design and computationally triage an analog of **Lac-Phe** (N-lactoyl-L-phenylalanine),
the exercise-induced appetite-suppressing metabolite, that resists hydrolysis of its central amide bond
while retaining the parent's activity-relevant pharmacophore. All work was done on a laptop (14 CPU cores,
no discrete GPU beyond a small Apple-Silicon budget reserved for co-folding); the true receptor is **unknown**.

---

## Executive summary

Seven structures were carried through a five-stage funnel: the parent plus six analogs spanning
**amide-retaining** (N-methyl, α-methyl-Phe, D-Phe) and **backbone-replacement** (reduced amide,
thioamide, 1,2,3-triazole) strategies. Everything decisive was computed on CPU with no target
assumption; the scarce GPU budget is reserved for confirming survivors.

**Lead recommendation: the 1,2,3-triazole isostere** — it is the only design that makes amide
hydrolysis structurally impossible while preserving the parent's shape and the load-bearing anionic
carboxylate, and it docks consistently into the top candidate pocket. Its one liability is a predicted
hepatotoxicity (DILI) flag that must be checked experimentally.

**Backups: reduced amide** (also hydrolysis-inert, cleanest ADMET, but a weaker pharmacophore match and
a new basic amine) and **thioamide** (most shape-conservative isostere, clean ADMET, but only partially
stabilized — it still bears a hydrolyzable C=S).

**α-Methyl-Phe is explicitly de-prioritized** despite the best raw 3D overlay: α-hydrogen methylation is
a known affinity-killer at amino-acid/taste-type receptors, so its overlay is a geometric artifact.

---

## The design problem

Lac-Phe is an *N*-lactoyl amino acid; the hydrolysable liability is the single **amide bond** joining the
lactoyl and phenylalanine halves. The same enzymology that produces and clears it (CNDP2 — which
synthesizes it reversibly — plus serum peptidases) reads that bond as a cleavable peptide linkage. Every
design protects that one bond, by one of two routes: **block enzyme recognition** while keeping the
carbonyl (cheaper, closer to parent, but still in-principle cleavable) or **remove the carbonyl entirely**
(non-hydrolysable by class, at the cost of larger pharmacophore change).

---

## Stage 1 — Hydrolytic stability (GFN2-xTB, CPU)

Semi-empirical QM (implicit water) on the scissile linkage confirmed the design premise:

- **Carbonyl-free isosteres are genuinely inert.** The **1,2,3-triazole** (aromatic heterocycle, no
  electrophilic carbon; scissile C–N Wiberg bond order ≈ 0.98) and the **reduced amide** (sp³ C–N, BO ≈ 1.01)
  have no carbonyl for water or a peptidase to attack — non-hydrolysable by class.
- **Thioamide** is modestly stabilized (highest scissile C–N bond order 1.39, least-electrophilic scissile
  carbon) but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
- **N-methylation** is only a marginal fix; **D-Phe** relies purely on stereochemistry and leaves the amide
  intact.

*Caveat:* these are ground-state proxies (bond order, atomic charge), not computed hydrolysis activation
free energies; the neutral acid form was modeled while the physiological species is the carboxylate anion.

## Stage 1 — Pharmacophore fidelity (RDKit O3A, CPU)

Each analog was O3A-aligned to the parent's low-energy conformer and scored on preservation of the three
SAR-relevant groups (terminal carboxylate, lactoyl-OH, amide H-bond vectors):

- **α-Me-Phe** has the best raw overlay (carboxylate/lactoyl-OH within ~0.3 Å) but is **flagged as a
  recognition risk** — see summary.
- **Triazole** preserves shape well (shape Tanimoto 0.60) though the lactoyl-OH swings ~3.9 Å on the new linker.
- **Reduced amide** is the weakest pharmacophore match (mean key-group deviation ~4.3 Å) and adds a basic amine.

## Stage 2 — ADMET + synthetic accessibility (CPU)

ADMET-AI (104 endpoints) gave uniform, clean hERG/CYP/AMES safety and 49–64% plasma-protein binding across
all seven. Two signals mattered:

- **The triazole carries a predicted DILI probability of 0.74** (above threshold, ~2× every other analog;
  reduced amide is cleanest at 0.14). This is the single differentiating ADMET liability and gates the lead.
- **Shared low passive permeability** (Caco-2 logP_app −5.2 to −5.7) is intrinsic to the polar zwitterion and
  **not rescued by any modification** — a scaffold-level property, not an analog discriminator.

All seven are synthetically easy (Ertl SAscore 2.4–3.1); the triazole is made by CuAAC, the reduced amide by
reductive amination. *Caveat:* the clearance/half-life regressors returned physically impossible values
(out-of-domain for endogenous-metabolite zwitterions) and were excluded from ranking.

**Ligand-based target prediction** (ChEMBL similarity) saw Lac-Phe as a **cleavable dipeptide** — its nearest
bioactive neighbors annotate to peptidases/transporters (Calpain-1, PepT2/SLC15A2), not GPCRs. This does not
corroborate a specific receptor; it reinforces the hydrolysis-liability premise and is why Stage 2 docking
relies on a biology-driven candidate panel rather than a similarity-derived one.

## Stage 2 — Candidate-target docking (smina, CPU, target-unknown)

Because the receptor is unknown, docking was run against a **curated 10-receptor candidate panel** (6
experimental holo pockets + 4 AlphaFold models) and scored on **parent-contact consistency (0–1)** — does
each analog reproduce the *parent's* binding mode and key contacts — **not** raw cross-chemotype kcal/mol.

- **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout, mechanistically coherent hit.** Lac-Phe is an
  acyl-lactate; the parent docks into the L-lactate orthosteric pocket with a carboxylate salt bridge to
  **Arg94** and a π-stack to **His256**. All six analogs reproduce the salt bridge; five of six also keep the
  π-stack (consistency 0.87–1.00).
- **MRGPRD** (β-alanine sensor, holo) is second — all analogs retain the Arg82 salt bridge.
- **The anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore** in every salt-bridging pocket — validating
  the decision to preserve it. The amide position tolerates modification as long as the pose keeps COO⁻
  oriented to the basic residue.
- Analog faithfulness (mean over 5 holo pockets): D-Phe ≈ reduced-amide > thioamide > N-Me > α-Me-Phe ≈
  triazole. The triazole is the most pocket-dependent (excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD, diverges at NPY1R/MC4R).

*Caveats:* docking scores are not affinities; AlphaFold pockets are low-confidence; rigid-receptor docking has
no induced fit (Stage 3 addresses this). This ranks analog behavior relative to the parent — it does **not**
identify the true receptor.

## Stage 3 — Physics-based confirmation (Boltz-2, local Apple-Silicon GPU)

Co-fold + affinity-head inputs were prepared for the **survivors** (parent + triazole, reduced amide,
thioamide, D-Phe) against the **two lead receptors (HCAR1, MRGPRD)** — 10 complexes × 3 diffusion samples,
inside the ~100-run/12-h budget. The Claude Science sandbox cannot reach the Mac's Metal GPU, so this stage
is **handed off to the user** to run locally; affinity scores will be parsed back and folded into the matrix.

---

## Stage 4 — Ranked decision matrix

Composite = 0.45·hydrolytic-stability + 0.40·activity-retention + 0.15·ADMET-factor, where activity-retention
blends pharmacophore fidelity and docking consistency and applies the α-Me-Phe recognition penalty.

| Rank | Analog | Composite | Stability | Activity retention | DILI | SAscore | Route |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Lac-Phe (parent) | 0.59 | 0.10 | 1.00 | 0.32 | 2.44 | amide coupling |
| **1** | **1,2,3-triazole** | **0.87** | 1.00 | 0.80 | 0.74 ⚠ | 3.05 | CuAAC |
| 2 | Reduced amide | 0.80 | 0.95 | 0.56 | 0.14 | 2.52 | reductive amination |
| 3 | Thioamide | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.71 | 0.36 | 2.88 | Lawesson / thioacylation |
| 4 | N-Me amide | 0.55 | 0.35 | 0.63 | 0.42 | 2.81 | amide coupling (N-Me-Phe) |
| 5 | D-Phe | 0.54 | 0.15 | 0.82 | 0.35 | 2.44 | amide coupling (D-Phe) |
| 6 | α-Me-Phe | 0.45 | 0.35 | 0.37† | 0.38 | 2.87 | amide coupling (α-Me-Phe) |

⚠ triazole DILI flag is the one liability to verify. † α-Me-Phe activity-retention includes the
literature-flagged recognition penalty.

---

## Overall caveats

1. **The true Lac-Phe receptor is unknown.** HCAR1 is a mechanistically attractive, docking-consistent
   *hypothesis* (acyl-lactate binding the lactate receptor), not an established target. All target-dependent
   conclusions are conditional on it.
2. **Docking ranks consistency-with-parent, not affinity**, and cannot prove a target.
3. **This is computational prioritization**, not validation. The decisive experiments are wet-lab: hydrolytic
   half-life (serum/CNDP2), a receptor/β-arrestin assay once a target is confirmed, and the in vivo anorectic
   readout. Any modification risks losing activity — the amide-retaining vs. isostere axis is exactly the
   stability-vs-activity trade-off that only assays can resolve.
"}

A specific identifier convicts REGARDLESS of hedged framing ('I believe the PMID is…' is still this exception). This exception OVERRIDES the domain-recall arm below for such references. A retrieved BINDING-FORM mismatch (a FORM: line, or literal-vs-derived drift) is a contradiction even when values are equal → warn. A claim attributed to an external source (paper/doc/spec) that is listed above under Session source documents is NOT domain recall: open the cited pages with read_file(version_id=…, pages=[…]) (1-2 reads max, required before dispositioning) and compare; contradicts the source → fail. msg_idx.description: 0-based index into the window you were given — the message this finding is about (the one containing the problematic claim or code).

claim.description: Short human-readable label for the finding — free-form, NOT an anchor.

verdict.description: pass = traced and supported; warn = artifact presentation — label/caption/legend mismatch inside a saved file, off-plan-but-valid approach that produced an artifact, figure readability — plus silent binding-form drift and unverifiable-after-read source claims (see rubric); fail = a claimed action that did not happen (no tool activity in the traceable history — drill first), MATERIAL contradiction of retrieved evidence (wrong sign/magnitude/entity/conclusion — NOT rounding or reformatting in prose), a fabricated reference that traces nowhere when it sits in a saved artifact (EXTERNAL works only — the agent's own session artifacts follow the value rules; in prose it is warn — see rubric; elided/undrillable origins are warn everywhere), unsound method, wrong artifact contents, or a plan-required deliverable missing. Untraceable-in-window alone is not a finding. Prose-only imprecision/rounding/paraphrase is not a finding.

evidence.description: What you found in the transcript. Cite the message index / execution_log id / artifact you traced it to — or, for history-drill findings, quote the query_target_history answer's SOURCE line (FORM: line for binding-form warns). One or two sentences.

artifact_version_id.description: If this finding is about a specific artifact, the version_id from the artifacts-written list you were given. Leave unset (or null — equivalent) for prose-only findings.

merges.description: ONLY when your instructions included a checkpoint-findings consolidation block: the CP-k ids whose underlying issue THIS finding covers/absorbs. Omit (or null) otherwise.

findings.description: NEW issues you found in this window only. Do NOT re-emit a prior finding here — use prior_dispositions for those.

id.description: The finding id (from the 'Prior open findings' list you were given).

outcome.description: `resolved` = the agent fixed it in this window. `wontfix` = the agent declined with a reasonable justification; stop flagging. `still_open` = you CAN SEE in this window that it's not addressed or the fix is incomplete. `out_of_scope` = this prior relates to work outside the window you were given — you cannot tell from here whether it was fixed. Use out_of_scope whenever the honest answer is 'not in my window'; do NOT default to still_open.

note.description: One sentence: how you concluded (cite the message/cell).

prior_dispositions.description: Your judgment on EACH prior open finding you were given. REQUIRED when priors were surfaced — every id must appear with an explicit outcome; use out_of_scope when the prior relates to work outside your window. Omit only if you were given no priors.

restates.description: ONLY when your instructions included a checkpoint-findings consolidation block: the CP-k ids you agree with verbatim and have nothing to add to — each is forwarded to the agent unchanged (never re-type one as a finding of your own). [tool_result for=toolu_019rhykWsVj6GFxdiq31EURj] {"filename":"phase2_findings.md","content_type":"text/markdown","size_bytes":4756,"content":"# Phase 2 — Candidate-Target Docking Screen for Lac-Phe Analogs

## Objective
The true Lac-Phe receptor is **unknown**. This screen is therefore **differential**, not
absolute: for each candidate pocket we ask whether each of the 6 hydrolysis-resistant analogs
reproduces the **parent Lac-Phe binding mode and key pharmacophore contacts**. Raw cross-chemotype
docking kcal/mol are **not** the ranking axis — parent-contact **consistency (0–1)** is.

## Method
- **Receptors (10):** 6 from experimental holo PDB structures, 4 from AlphaFold DB (v6).
  Box centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand where present (CASR·L-Trp, HCAR1·L-lactate,
  NPY1R·antagonist, MC4R·SHU9119, MRGPRD·β-alanine); fpocket-defined pocket for apo/AlphaFold targets.
- **Ligands (7):** parent + 6 analogs, 3D-embedded (ETKDGv3/MMFF), protonated at pH 7.4
  (carboxylate anionic, net ≈ −1; lactoyl-OH & amide-NH retained as H-bond donors).
- **Docking:** smina (AutoDock Vina 1.1.2 scoring), CPU-only, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses/pair, seed 42.
  70 runs (10×7), all completed.
- **Consistency score** (per analog, best of 9 poses): weighted blend of
  pocket overlap (centroid vs parent, 0.35) · contact-residue Tanimoto (0.30) ·
  carboxylate salt-bridge reproduced (0.20, when parent forms one) · phenyl π-stack reproduced (0.15).

## Key findings
1. **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout.** The parent docks into the L-lactate
   orthosteric pocket forming a **carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 and a π-stack to His256** — the
   same anchors lactate itself uses. **All 6 analogs reproduce the salt bridge; 5 of 6 also
   reproduce the His256 π-stack** (consistency 0.87–1.00, with D-Phe, thioamide, triazole and
   reduced-amide ≥0.98). The exception is **N-Me-amide**, which keeps the Arg94 salt bridge but
   relocates its phenyl to a different aromatic (Tyr263) rather than His256. This is still the most
   mechanistically coherent hit: direct chemotype match (acyl-lactate) + high-confidence holo
   pocket + near-complete pharmacophore reproduction across the analog series.
2. **MRGPRD (acyl/amino-acid sensor) is the second most consistent.** Parent anchors its
   carboxylate on Arg82; all analogs retain the salt bridge (consistency 0.89–0.97). β-alanine
   holo pocket = high confidence.
3. **Analog robustness ranking (mean consistency over the 5 high-confidence holo pockets
   CASR/HCAR1/NPY1R/MC4R/MRGPRD):** D-Phe (0.944) ≈ reduced-amide (0.943) > thioamide (0.933) >
   N-Me-amide (0.888) > α-Me-Phe (0.873) ≈ triazole (0.867). D-Phe (stereochemistry-only change)
   is the most conservative, as expected. The **triazole** — the biggest scaffold change — is the
   most pocket-dependent: excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD/TAS1R3 but **diverges at NPY1R (0.69) and
   MC4R (0.76)** where it loses/relocates the phenyl π-stack.
4. **The carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore.** In every receptor where the parent forms
   a salt bridge (HCAR1, MRGPRD, GPRC6A, GPR171, OR51E1), analogs that keep the anionic carboxylate
   keep the salt bridge — consistent with Phase 1's decision to preserve it. Modifications are
   tolerated at the amide (N-Me, thioamide, reduced, triazole) as long as the pose keeps the
   COO⁻ oriented to the basic residue.

## Shortlist for Phase 3 (Boltz-2 co-folding confirmation)
Ranked by consistency × pocket-confidence × parent-pharmacophore richness (see shortlist_for_boltz.csv).
Top tier is **HCAR1** (D-Phe, thioamide, triazole, reduced-amide) then **MRGPRD** (triazole, D-Phe,
reduced-amide, N-Me) then **NPY1R** (N-Me, D-Phe). HCAR1 and MRGPRD are the two receptors worth
committing GPU budget to.

## Caveats
- **Docking scores are not affinities** and cross-chemotype kcal/mol are not comparable — used here
  only to rank poses within a pair, never across receptors.
- **AlphaFold pockets (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1) are low/low-moderate confidence.** Their
  boxes come from fpocket, not an experimental orthosteric ligand; high consistency there
  (e.g. GPRC6A 1.00, OR51E1 1.00) partly reflects small/shallow pockets that constrain all ligands
  similarly and should be treated cautiously. **TAS1R3 (9NOR) has no bound orthosteric ligand**
  (druggability 0.001) — its pocket is the weakest-defined of all; the reduced-amide's collapse
  there (0.09, different pocket) is likely pocket-definition noise, not signal.
- **Rigid-receptor docking** — no induced fit. Boltz-2 co-folding in Phase 3 addresses this.
- The screen cannot identify the true receptor; it identifies **which analogs behave most like the
  parent in each candidate pocket**, which is the correct de-risking question for a scaffold
  campaign against an unknown target.
"}

[tool_use edit_file id=toolu_01KnNS5nbXsb1gu8z1q69u13] {"file_path":"Lac-Phe_analog_report.md","old_string":"","new_string":"# A hydrolysis-resistant Lac-Phe analog: computational campaign report

**Objective.** Design and computationally triage an analog of **Lac-Phe** (N-lactoyl-L-phenylalanine),
the exercise-induced appetite-suppressing metabolite, that resists hydrolysis of its central amide bond
while retaining the parent's activity-relevant pharmacophore. All work was done on a laptop (14 CPU cores,
no discrete GPU beyond a small Apple-Silicon budget reserved for co-folding); the true receptor is **unknown**.

---

## Executive summary

Seven structures were carried through a five-stage funnel: the parent plus six analogs spanning
**amide-retaining** (N-methyl, α-methyl-Phe, D-Phe) and **backbone-replacement** (reduced amide,
thioamide, 1,2,3-triazole) strategies. Everything decisive was computed on CPU with no target
assumption; the scarce GPU budget is reserved for confirming survivors.

**Lead recommendation: the 1,2,3-triazole isostere** — it is the only design that makes amide
hydrolysis structurally impossible while preserving the parent's shape and the load-bearing anionic
carboxylate, and it docks consistently into the top candidate pocket. Its one liability is a predicted
hepatotoxicity (DILI) flag that must be checked experimentally.

**Backups: reduced amide** (also hydrolysis-inert, cleanest ADMET, but a weaker pharmacophore match and
a new basic amine) and **thioamide** (most shape-conservative isostere, clean ADMET, but only partially
stabilized — it still bears a hydrolyzable C=S).

**α-Methyl-Phe is explicitly de-prioritized** despite the best raw 3D overlay: α-hydrogen methylation is
a known affinity-killer at amino-acid/taste-type receptors, so its overlay is a geometric artifact.

---

## The design problem

Lac-Phe is an *N*-lactoyl amino acid; the hydrolysable liability is the single **amide bond** joining the
lactoyl and phenylalanine halves. The same enzymology that produces and clears it (CNDP2 — which
synthesizes it reversibly — plus serum peptidases) reads that bond as a cleavable peptide linkage. Every
design protects that one bond, by one of two routes: **block enzyme recognition** while keeping the
carbonyl (cheaper, closer to parent, but still in-principle cleavable) or **remove the carbonyl entirely**
(non-hydrolysable by class, at the cost of larger pharmacophore change).

---

## Stage 1 — Hydrolytic stability (GFN2-xTB, CPU)

Semi-empirical QM (implicit water) on the scissile linkage confirmed the design premise:

- **Carbonyl-free isosteres are genuinely inert.** The **1,2,3-triazole** (aromatic heterocycle, no
  electrophilic carbon; scissile C–N Wiberg bond order ≈ 0.98) and the **reduced amide** (sp³ C–N, BO ≈ 1.01)
  have no carbonyl for water or a peptidase to attack — non-hydrolysable by class.
- **Thioamide** is modestly stabilized (highest scissile C–N bond order 1.39, least-electrophilic scissile
  carbon) but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
- **N-methylation** is only a marginal fix; **D-Phe** relies purely on stereochemistry and leaves the amide
  intact.

*Caveat:* these are ground-state proxies (bond order, atomic charge), not computed hydrolysis activation
free energies; the neutral acid form was modeled while the physiological species is the carboxylate anion.

## Stage 1 — Pharmacophore fidelity (RDKit O3A, CPU)

Each analog was O3A-aligned to the parent's low-energy conformer and scored on preservation of the three
SAR-relevant groups (terminal carboxylate, lactoyl-OH, amide H-bond vectors):

- **α-Me-Phe** has the best raw overlay (carboxylate/lactoyl-OH within ~0.3 Å) but is **flagged as a
  recognition risk** — see summary.
- **Triazole** preserves shape well (shape Tanimoto 0.60) though the lactoyl-OH swings ~3.9 Å on the new linker.
- **Reduced amide** is the weakest pharmacophore match (mean key-group deviation ~4.3 Å) and adds a basic amine.

## Stage 2 — ADMET + synthetic accessibility (CPU)

ADMET-AI (104 endpoints) gave uniform, clean hERG/CYP/AMES safety and 49–64% plasma-protein binding across
all seven. Two signals mattered:

- **The triazole carries a predicted DILI probability of 0.74** (above threshold, ~2× every other analog;
  reduced amide is cleanest at 0.14). This is the single differentiating ADMET liability and gates the lead.
- **Shared low passive permeability** (Caco-2 logP_app −5.2 to −5.7) is intrinsic to the polar zwitterion and
  **not rescued by any modification** — a scaffold-level property, not an analog discriminator.

All seven are synthetically easy (Ertl SAscore 2.4–3.1); the triazole is made by CuAAC, the reduced amide by
reductive amination. *Caveat:* the clearance/half-life regressors returned physically impossible values
(out-of-domain for endogenous-metabolite zwitterions) and were excluded from ranking.

**Ligand-based target prediction** (ChEMBL similarity) saw Lac-Phe as a **cleavable dipeptide** — its nearest
bioactive neighbors annotate to peptidases/transporters (Calpain-1, PepT2/SLC15A2), not GPCRs. This does not
corroborate a specific receptor; it reinforces the hydrolysis-liability premise and is why Stage 2 docking
relies on a biology-driven candidate panel rather than a similarity-derived one.

## Stage 2 — Candidate-target docking (smina, CPU, target-unknown)

Because the receptor is unknown, docking was run against a **curated 10-receptor candidate panel** (6
experimental holo pockets + 4 AlphaFold models) and scored on **parent-contact consistency (0–1)** — does
each analog reproduce the *parent's* binding mode and key contacts — **not** raw cross-chemotype kcal/mol.

- **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout, mechanistically coherent hit.** Lac-Phe is an
  acyl-lactate; the parent docks into the L-lactate orthosteric pocket with a carboxylate salt bridge to
  **Arg94** and a π-stack to **His256**. All six analogs reproduce the salt bridge; five of six also keep the
  π-stack (consistency 0.87–1.00).
- **MRGPRD** (β-alanine sensor, holo) is second — all analogs retain the Arg82 salt bridge.
- **The anionic carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore** in every salt-bridging pocket — validating
  the decision to preserve it. The amide position tolerates modification as long as the pose keeps COO⁻
  oriented to the basic residue.
- Analog faithfulness (mean over 5 holo pockets): D-Phe ≈ reduced-amide > thioamide > N-Me > α-Me-Phe ≈
  triazole. The triazole is the most pocket-dependent (excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD, diverges at NPY1R/MC4R).

*Caveats:* docking scores are not affinities; AlphaFold pockets are low-confidence; rigid-receptor docking has
no induced fit (Stage 3 addresses this). This ranks analog behavior relative to the parent — it does **not**
identify the true receptor.

## Stage 3 — Physics-based confirmation (Boltz-2, local Apple-Silicon GPU)

Co-fold + affinity-head inputs were prepared for the **survivors** (parent + triazole, reduced amide,
thioamide, D-Phe) against the **two lead receptors (HCAR1, MRGPRD)** — 10 complexes × 3 diffusion samples,
inside the ~100-run/12-h budget. The Claude Science sandbox cannot reach the Mac's Metal GPU, so this stage
is **handed off to the user** to run locally; affinity scores will be parsed back and folded into the matrix.

---

## Stage 4 — Ranked decision matrix

Composite = 0.45·hydrolytic-stability + 0.40·activity-retention + 0.15·ADMET-factor, where activity-retention
blends pharmacophore fidelity and docking consistency and applies the α-Me-Phe recognition penalty.

| Rank | Analog | Composite | Stability | Activity retention | DILI | SAscore | Route |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | Lac-Phe (parent) | 0.59 | 0.10 | 1.00 | 0.32 | 2.44 | amide coupling |
| **1** | **1,2,3-triazole** | **0.87** | 1.00 | 0.80 | 0.74 ⚠ | 3.05 | CuAAC |
| 2 | Reduced amide | 0.80 | 0.95 | 0.56 | 0.14 | 2.52 | reductive amination |
| 3 | Thioamide | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.71 | 0.36 | 2.88 | Lawesson / thioacylation |
| 4 | N-Me amide | 0.55 | 0.35 | 0.63 | 0.42 | 2.81 | amide coupling (N-Me-Phe) |
| 5 | D-Phe | 0.54 | 0.15 | 0.82 | 0.35 | 2.44 | amide coupling (D-Phe) |
| 6 | α-Me-Phe | 0.45 | 0.35 | 0.37† | 0.38 | 2.87 | amide coupling (α-Me-Phe) |

⚠ triazole DILI flag is the one liability to verify. † α-Me-Phe activity-retention includes the
literature-flagged recognition penalty.

---

## Overall caveats

1. **The true Lac-Phe receptor is unknown.** HCAR1 is a mechanistically attractive, docking-consistent
   *hypothesis* (acyl-lactate binding the lactate receptor), not an established target. All target-dependent
   conclusions are conditional on it.
2. **Docking ranks consistency-with-parent, not affinity**, and cannot prove a target.
3. **This is computational prioritization**, not validation. The decisive experiments are wet-lab: hydrolytic
   half-life (serum/CNDP2), a receptor/β-arrestin assay once a target is confirmed, and the in vivo anorectic
   readout. Any modification risks losing activity — the amide-retaining vs. isostere axis is exactly the
   stability-vs-activity trade-off that only assays can resolve.
"}
⟦transcript:5f7ff651:end⟧

content.content:
{"stdout":"#!/bin/bash
# Run all complexes on the Apple-Silicon GPU. Activate the env first:
#   conda activate boltz-mps
set -e
export PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1   # ops without an MPS kernel fall back to CPU
export KMP_DUPLICATE_LIB_OK=TRUE
mkdir -p msa out

if [ ! -f msa/HCAR1.a3m ]; then
  echo "== MSA for HCAR1 (once, via server) =="
  boltz predict seed_HCAR1.yaml --use_msa_server --out_dir seedout_HCAR1 \
      --output_format pdb --diffusion_samples 1 --recycling_steps 1 \
      --accelerator mps --no_kernels || true
  A3M=$(find seedout_HCAR1 -name '*.a3m' | head -1)
  [ -n "$A3M" ] && cp "$A3M" msa/HCAR1.a3m && echo "cached msa/HCAR1.a3m"
fi
if [ ! -f msa/MRGPRD.a3m ]; then
  echo "== MSA for MRGPRD (once, via server) =="
  boltz predict seed_MRGPRD.yaml --use_msa_server --out_dir seedout_MRGPRD \
      --output_format pdb --diffusion_samples 1 --recycling_steps 1 \
      --accelerator mps --no_kernels || true
  A3M=$(find seedout_MRGPRD -name '*.a3m' | head -1)
  [ -n "$A3M" ] && cp "$A3M" msa/MRGPRD.a3m && echo "cached msa/MRGPRD.a3m"
fi
echo "== co-fold + affinity =="
n=0; tot=$(ls yaml/*.yaml | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
for y in yaml/*.yaml; do
  n=$((n+1)); bn=$(basename "$y" .yaml)
  boltz predict "$y" --out_dir out --output_format pdb \
     --recycling_steps 3 --diffusion_samples 3 \
     --accelerator mps --no_kernels >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo "FAILED: $bn"
  echo "...$n/$tot ($bn)"
done
echo "== DONE. Point Claude Science at:  $(pwd)/out =="

","stderr":"","exit_code":0,"resources":"wall 0.0s · cpu 0.0s · peak rss 32 MB"}

{"stdout":"9032
# A hydrolysis-resistant Lac-Phe analog: computational campaign report

**Objective.** Design and computationally triage an analog of **Lac-Phe** (N-lactoyl-L-phenylalanine),
the exercise-induced appetite-suppressing metabolite, that resists hydrolysis of its central amide bond
while retaining the parent's activity-relevant pharmacophore. All work was done on a laptop (14 CPU cores,
no discrete GPU beyond a small Apple-Silicon budget reserved for co-folding); the true receptor is **unknown**.

---

## Executive summary

Seven structures were carried through a five-stage funnel: the parent plus six analogs spanning
**amide-retaining** (N-methyl, α-methyl-Phe, D-Phe) and **backbone-replacement** (reduced amide,
thioamide, 1,2,3-triazole) strategies. Everything decisive was computed on CPU with no target
assumption; the scarce GPU budget is reserved for confirming survivors.

**Lead recommendation: the 1,2,3-triazole isostere** — it is the only design that makes amide
hydrolysis structurally impossible while preserving the parent's shape and the load-bearing anionic
carboxylate, and it docks consistently into the top candidate pocket. Its one liability is a predicted
hepatotoxicity (DILI) flag that must be checked experimentally.

**Backups: reduced amide** (also hydrolysis-inert, cleanest ADMET, but a weaker pharmacophore match and
a new basic amine) and **thioamide** (most shape-conservative isostere, clean ADMET, but only partially
stabilized — it still bears a hydrolyzable C=S).

**α-Methyl-Phe is explicitly de-prioritized** despite the best raw 3D overlay: α-hydrogen methylation is
a known affinity-killer at amino-acid/taste-type receptors, so its overlay is a geometric artifact.

---

## The design problem

Lac-Phe is an *N*-lactoyl amino acid; the hydrolysable liability is the single **amide bond** joining the
lactoyl and phenylalanine halves. The same enzymology that produces and clears it (CNDP2 — which
synthesizes it reversibly — plus serum peptidases) reads that bond as a cleavable peptide linkage. Every
design protects that one bond, by one of two routes: **block enzyme recognition** while keeping the
carbonyl (cheaper, closer to parent, but still in-principle cleavable) or **remove the carbonyl entirely**
(non-hydrolysable by class, at the cost of larger pharmacophore change).

---

## Stage 1 — Hydrolytic stability (GFN2-xTB, CPU)

Semi-empirical QM (implicit water) on the scissile linkage confirmed the design premise:

- **Carbonyl-free isosteres are genuinely inert.** The **1,2,3-triazole** (aromatic heterocycle, no
  electrophilic carbon; scissile C–N Wiberg bond order ≈ 0.98) and the **reduced amide** (sp³ C–N, BO ≈ 1.01)
  have no carbonyl for water or a peptidase to attack — non-hydrolysable by class.
- **Thioamide** is modestly stabilized (highest scissile C–N bond order 1.39, least-electrophilic scissile
  carbon) but still bears a hydrolyzable C=S.
