content.content:
{"filename":"phase2_findings.md","content_type":"text/markdown","size_bytes":4756,"content":"# Phase 2 — Candidate-Target Docking Screen for Lac-Phe Analogs

## Objective
The true Lac-Phe receptor is **unknown**. This screen is therefore **differential**, not
absolute: for each candidate pocket we ask whether each of the 6 hydrolysis-resistant analogs
reproduces the **parent Lac-Phe binding mode and key pharmacophore contacts**. Raw cross-chemotype
docking kcal/mol are **not** the ranking axis — parent-contact **consistency (0–1)** is.

## Method
- **Receptors (10):** 6 from experimental holo PDB structures, 4 from AlphaFold DB (v6).
  Box centered on the crystallographic orthosteric ligand where present (CASR·L-Trp, HCAR1·L-lactate,
  NPY1R·antagonist, MC4R·SHU9119, MRGPRD·β-alanine); fpocket-defined pocket for apo/AlphaFold targets.
- **Ligands (7):** parent + 6 analogs, 3D-embedded (ETKDGv3/MMFF), protonated at pH 7.4
  (carboxylate anionic, net ≈ −1; lactoyl-OH & amide-NH retained as H-bond donors).
- **Docking:** smina (AutoDock Vina 1.1.2 scoring), CPU-only, exhaustiveness 12, 9 poses/pair, seed 42.
  70 runs (10×7), all completed.
- **Consistency score** (per analog, best of 9 poses): weighted blend of
  pocket overlap (centroid vs parent, 0.35) · contact-residue Tanimoto (0.30) ·
  carboxylate salt-bridge reproduced (0.20, when parent forms one) · phenyl π-stack reproduced (0.15).

## Key findings
1. **HCAR1 (lactate receptor / GPR81) is the standout.** The parent docks into the L-lactate
   orthosteric pocket forming a **carboxylate salt bridge to Arg94 and a π-stack to His256** — the
   same anchors lactate itself uses. **All 6 analogs reproduce the salt bridge; 5 of 6 also
   reproduce the His256 π-stack** (consistency 0.87–1.00, with D-Phe, thioamide, triazole and
   reduced-amide ≥0.98). The exception is **N-Me-amide**, which keeps the Arg94 salt bridge but
   relocates its phenyl to a different aromatic (Tyr263) rather than His256. This is still the most
   mechanistically coherent hit: direct chemotype match (acyl-lactate) + high-confidence holo
   pocket + near-complete pharmacophore reproduction across the analog series.
2. **MRGPRD (acyl/amino-acid sensor) is the second most consistent.** Parent anchors its
   carboxylate on Arg82; all analogs retain the salt bridge (consistency 0.89–0.97). β-alanine
   holo pocket = high confidence.
3. **Analog robustness ranking (mean consistency over the 5 high-confidence holo pockets
   CASR/HCAR1/NPY1R/MC4R/MRGPRD):** D-Phe (0.944) ≈ reduced-amide (0.943) > thioamide (0.933) >
   N-Me-amide (0.888) > α-Me-Phe (0.873) ≈ triazole (0.867). D-Phe (stereochemistry-only change)
   is the most conservative, as expected. The **triazole** — the biggest scaffold change — is the
   most pocket-dependent: excellent at HCAR1/MRGPRD/TAS1R3 but **diverges at NPY1R (0.69) and
   MC4R (0.76)** where it loses/relocates the phenyl π-stack.
4. **The carboxylate is the load-bearing pharmacophore.** In every receptor where the parent forms
   a salt bridge (HCAR1, MRGPRD, GPRC6A, GPR171, OR51E1), analogs that keep the anionic carboxylate
   keep the salt bridge — consistent with Phase 1's decision to preserve it. Modifications are
   tolerated at the amide (N-Me, thioamide, reduced, triazole) as long as the pose keeps the
   COO⁻ oriented to the basic residue.

## Shortlist for Phase 3 (Boltz-2 co-folding confirmation)
Ranked by consistency × pocket-confidence × parent-pharmacophore richness (see shortlist_for_boltz.csv).
Top tier is **HCAR1** (D-Phe, thioamide, triazole, reduced-amide) then **MRGPRD** (triazole, D-Phe,
reduced-amide, N-Me) then **NPY1R** (N-Me, D-Phe). HCAR1 and MRGPRD are the two receptors worth
committing GPU budget to.

## Caveats
- **Docking scores are not affinities** and cross-chemotype kcal/mol are not comparable — used here
  only to rank poses within a pair, never across receptors.
- **AlphaFold pockets (GPRC6A, TAS1R1, GPR171, OR51E1) are low/low-moderate confidence.** Their
  boxes come from fpocket, not an experimental orthosteric ligand; high consistency there
  (e.g. GPRC6A 1.00, OR51E1 1.00) partly reflects small/shallow pockets that constrain all ligands
  similarly and should be treated cautiously. **TAS1R3 (9NOR) has no bound orthosteric ligand**
  (druggability 0.001) — its pocket is the weakest-defined of all; the reduced-amide's collapse
  there (0.09, different pocket) is likely pocket-definition noise, not signal.
- **Rigid-receptor docking** — no induced fit. Boltz-2 co-folding in Phase 3 addresses this.
- The screen cannot identify the true receptor; it identifies **which analogs behave most like the
  parent in each candidate pocket**, which is the correct de-risking question for a scaffold
  campaign against an unknown target.
"}
